Content:
Your blog has a clear study structure and I like how you connect the Mainland Rednote success to Hong Kong Instagram problems. I also learned new details from your competitor examples, especially how Silk Tea uses Cantonese replies, polls, and local partnerships to make people feel included. That helps build a stronger picture of what “local storytelling” really looks like in practice, not just in theory.

One part I did not quite understand is your KPI choice. You say the main goal is lead generation, but the only KPI you choose is engagement rate. Let me try thinking it through this way. Engagement rate measures interest, but lead generation needs an action, such as website clicks, store visits, or sign ups. So it may be better to keep engagement rate as a supporting KPI, and add one lead KPI like link clicks, coupon redemptions, or loyalty registrations.

For data collection, you tracked likes and comments, but I suggest also tracking saves, shares, and Story metrics (e.g. completion rate, sticker taps like poll votes) because your strategy focuses on “content worth saving” and FOMO launches. That will make your study results match your actions more closely.

Your blog is clear and well structured, and it follows the required steps in a logical way. I learned new detail from your KPI choice because you include video completion rate inside engagement rate, which matches short video platforms like TikTok and Rednote. I used to think engagement was only likes and comments, but your definition makes the KPI more complete for young audiences.

One point I am not quite understand is how you will calculate the Sentiment Score. Let me try thinking about it in this way. You could explain if it is the percentage of positive comments minus negative comments, or a rating from a social listening tool. If you add one simple method, the reader can see how sentiment becomes measurable, not just an idea.

Your competitor analysis is a good choice, but it may become too broad. Maybe choose two to three content categories to compare, such as discounts, memes, and collaborations. Then your findings can directly guide actions like the challenge campaign or IP partners. Finally, your reporting plan is strong, and adding one dashboard screenshot example could make it even clearer.

Your proposal is clear and follows the required steps well. I learned from your blog that you are treating Facebook as the main channel and using engagement rate as the single KPI, which keeps the study focused and measurable. The target audience description also builds a good picture of who Starbucks HK wants to reach and why they might interact with posts.

One part I did not quite understand at first is the statement that Facebook is a key platform for people of all ages. Let me try thinking about it in this way. If your target audience is mainly 25 to 45 professionals, you could explain why Facebook is still the best platform for this group compared with Instagram. Adding one short reason such as they follow brand pages for promotions or they share posts in groups would make the logic stronger.

For data collection, you mention impressions, reach, and interactions, which is helpful. You could also clarify the time period you will study and how you will compare content types fairly, for example by using engagement rate per post.

Your blog gives a clear and structured plan, and I like how you connect Instagram activity with Google Trends to show both social buzz and real demand. I learned a new way to think about the funnel here, where Discovery can be high while Community Depth stays low, and your Murakami example explains this gap well.

I am not quite understand about the KPI Intent-to-Engagement Ratio at first, so I try to think about it like this. If comments and shares increase, people should also start searching the brand or collaboration more. But it may be hard to prove that the search is caused by engagement, because paid ads, news, or influencer posts can also push searches. You could explain the formula and time window, for example search change within 24 to 72 hours after a post.

For suggestions, you can define how you will measure sentiment in a simple way. Also, include one clear action example, like testing two content styles for the same collaboration and comparing engagement rate and search lift. This will make your recommendations feel more practical and reasonable.

Your blog is clear and well structured, and I learned a new way to frame Nintendo’s social media success by using sentiment distribution as the main KPI. I like that you connect Nintendo’s fan culture with emotions, because in gaming communities feelings can change very fast after events like Nintendo Direct.

One part I did not fully understand at first is how you will measure sentiment distribution in a reliable way. Let me try thinking about it like this. If you only label posts as positive, neutral, or negative, you may miss mixed emotions such as excited but worried. You could improve the knowledge building by explaining whether you will use a manual coding sample to check accuracy, and how you will handle sarcasm, memes, or short comments.

I also suggest adding one supporting metric such as engagement rate or volume of mentions. This helps you interpret sentiment better, because negative sentiment with low volume is different from negative sentiment with a sudden spike.

Your blog is clear and well structured, and it follows the steps from the requirements. I learned new details about how you connect Engagement Rate with Sentiment Analysis to measure whether people truly feel the brand is inclusive, not only whether they click like or comment. That helps build a more complete understanding of community connection.

I am not quite understand about the statement that three months of Instagram posts is fully representative. Let me try think about it in this way. Three months can show recent performance, but it might miss seasonal campaigns, product launches, or viral moments. You could strengthen the study by explaining why you chose three months, or by adding a comparison with the previous three months to see if patterns are stable.

I also suggest defining Engagement Rate clearly, because different tools calculate it differently. For example, is it engagement divided by reach, impressions, or followers. If you explain your formula, stakeholders can trust the results and repeat the study later.

Your blog is clear and well structured, and it helps me build a complete picture of how a social media analytics study can work for Time for Thai. I like that you connect the goal of brand awareness with a measurable KPI such as followers and engagement rate, because it makes success easier to track. The target audience description is also specific, especially linking students to UNSW and USYD and including the Muslim community, which fits well with your halal related positioning.

I was not quite understand at first how keyword frequency in 5 star reviews becomes an action plan, so I try to explain it this way. If many customers repeat words like Ko Mok Gai, roti, halal, or friendly staff, those repeated words become evidence of what people value most. Then you can compare that evidence with your current posts to see if you are promoting the same things customers praise.

One suggestion is to define a time period and sample size for reviews and social posts, so the findings feel more reliable. Also, for hygiene complaints, you could track sentiment over time after renovation to prove improvement, not only show photos.

Your blog is clear and well structured, and it follows the five steps in a logical way. I learned new and useful details from your plan, especially how you define the KPI as average post interaction rate and how you focus on young people in first tier cities. This helps build a complete picture of what success means for Starbucks China on social media, not only in sales but also in relationships with users.

One part I am not quite understand is the KPI formula. You divide by total followers, but followers can include inactive users, and different platforms may count followers differently. Let me try think about it in this way. If the goal is to compare post performance fairly, it may be better to also track engagement rate by reach or impressions, because it shows how many people who actually saw the post decided to interact.

I also suggest choosing one main analysis type as required, then use the other as support. For example, do content analysis as the core, then use trend analysis only to explain time changes. Finally, your reporting could include a simple dashboard with top content themes, best posting times, and three clear actions for the next month.

Your blog gives a clear and complete plan because you connect the campaign goal with one main KPI, conversion rate, and you also explain who the target audience is and where they spend time online. I learned a useful point from your data collection part that combining platform data with app store trend data can help show whether social content really leads to downloads and first orders, not only likes.

One statement I was not fully sure about is how you will calculate conversion rate. Let me try to explain it in a simple way. You can define it as downloads to first order, or link clicks to registrations, or invitation code uses to first order. If you choose one clear formula, stakeholders will understand the result faster and it will be easier to compare content categories.

Your content analysis categories are strong. A suggestion is to add one more layer that separates paid posts and organic posts, because reach and clicks can change a lot due to advertising. Also, for sentiment analysis, you can briefly explain how you will label comments as positive, neutral, or negative, so stakeholders will trust the results more.

Your blog is clear and well structured, and it explains why Citybus needs analytics after the rebranding. I learned a new way to connect branding with measurement, especially how you link zero emission positioning with the KPI engagement rate. It helps build a complete picture from goal to action, not just collecting numbers.

I am not quite understand about the engagement rate formula you wrote as likes plus comments plus shares divided by total followers. Let me try think about it in this way. If the follower base changes a lot, the same post performance may look better or worse. You could also test engagement rate by reach, which is interactions divided by reach, because it measures how interesting the post is to people who actually saw it.

Your competitor analysis idea is strong, but I suggest adding one more control point like posting time, format, and paid boosts, because those factors can create engagement spikes. For reporting, you can include two or three example posts each month to show why the numbers changed, so stakeholders can learn faster.

Your blog is clear and well structured. I also learned new brand specific context from you, especially how TamJai SamGor uses TamJai dialect and the Jeh Jeh characters to build local internet humor. That helps explain why engagement is a better goal than awareness for this brand.

I am not quite understand about the engagement rate formula part, because dividing by total followers per post can hide the effect of reach. Let me try thinking it in this way. If one post is shown to many non followers, it may look weak by your formula even if it performs well. You could add a second check like engagement per reach to confirm the result, while still keeping engagement rate as the main KPI.

Your content analysis idea is strong. As a suggestion, define your content categories in advance and include examples, then connect each category to a simple action rule. For reporting, add one short slide on limitations like seasonality and paid boosts so stakeholders trust the findings more.

Your blog is clear and well structured, and it follows the five required steps. I also learned new information from your numbers on QQ, especially that users under 35 are 62.9 percent and users 25 and below are 31.9 percent. This helps build a coherent picture that QQ is still strong with younger people

One part I am not quite understand is your KPI choice. You mention active user count, usage rates, age groups, and city distribution, but it is better to provide one KPI only to make the analysis more focused. Let me try think about it in this way. If your goal is to increase the active user base, then one KPI could be Monthly Active Users. Then the other numbers become supporting metrics that explain why Monthly Active Users (MAU) changes, not extra KPIs.

I also suggest adding a short explanation of why WeChat is the main comparison point, and what specific trend pattern would trigger action. For example, if MAU decline slows after a UI change, that supports your strategy. Finally, for reporting, consider adding one simple summary table with key findings and recommended actions so stakeholders can decide faster.

This blog gives a clear and practical plan for studying McDonald’s Instagram performance. I like that it follows the social media analytics steps in a logical order, starting from the goal, then data collection, analysis, action, and reporting. The focus on engagement rate is a good choice because it helps measure how well the audience is reacting to the content. The target audience is also clearly identified, which makes the study more realistic and easy to follow.

I am not quite understand about the statement that low posting volume causes follower loss, because this idea may be true, but it still needs stronger evidence. Let me try thinking about it in this way. Maybe the problem is not only how often McDonald’s posts, but also what kind of content they post and whether students find it interesting. It may help to compare both posting frequency and content quality together.

As a suggestion, the blog could explain more clearly how engagement rate will be calculated. It would also be useful to mention a time period for collecting data, such as one month or three months, to make the study more reliable and coherent.

This blog has a very interesting and emotional idea. I like how it connects social media analytics with the revival of physical music culture. The goal is clear, and the writing makes Tower Records feel more than just a shop. It feels like a cultural experience. This gives the study a strong direction.

I am not quite understand about the KPI part at first, so let me try think about it in this way. Topic volume means how many people are talking about Tower Records or physical music on social media. This is useful because it shows public interest before opening new stores. However, I think the blog could explain more clearly why topic volume is the best single KPI instead of engagement rate or sentiment. That would make the plan stronger.

I also think the content analysis choice is good because it helps discover what music style different cities prefer. Maybe the blog can add one example of how data from Instagram, Twitter, and website searches will be compared together. That would help readers understand the method better. Overall, this is a creative and well-focused proposal with strong potential.

This blog is clear and well organized, and it gives a good basic plan for studying Uniqlo’s social media performance. I like that the writer chose one brand, one main goal, and one KPI. That makes the study focused and easier to follow. The target audience is also explained in a simple way, so readers can understand why Instagram is the main platform.

I am not quite understand about one point, which is why only reach is used to measure brand awareness. Let me try to think about it in this way. Reach shows how many people see the content, but it does not fully show whether they care about it or remember it. Maybe the blog could briefly explain why reach is the best KPI for this study, or mention that engagement could support the result.

The trend analysis idea is strong because Uniqlo often has new launches and collaborations. I think the blog could be even better by adding one example campaign and explaining how the findings would help future content planning. This would make the study more practical and easier to imagine.

This post is strong as a social-media analytics brief: it defines a clear business goal (re-engagement and loyalty) and measurable KPIs (reach/sentiment, community growth, completion rate). It proposes a multi-platform data pipeline (Bilibili, Douyin, streaming sites, plus HSReplay/HSGuru and NetEase Dashen) that covers both conversation and gameplay meta signals. The chosen method—trend analysis mapped to campaign phases and balance patches—helps distinguish short-term reward-driven returns from sustained interest. Action items are practical (optimize deck tutorial content, run community events, increase transparent dev communication), and the reporting plan is stakeholder-friendly (slides with charts/tables).

This plan is strong because it aligns tactics with Starbucks HK’s maturity stage: it prioritizes engagement/community over awareness. It uses a clear, interpretable KPI (Engagement Rate) and a realistic data source (Instagram Insights) that matches the target demographic. Recommended actions are concrete—shift content mix, invest in influencer/UGC contests—and the monthly dashboard format makes results easy for stakeholders to monitor and iterate quickly.

Bilibili functions as a tightly knit interest-based graph where mid-tier creators act as key nodes sustaining engagement. By analyzing comments/danmaku, it maps tie strength and community norms that drive “one-click triple” behavior. Incentives and algorithms reshape diffusion, retention, and creator churn.

UNIQLO’s strategy leverages platform-specific graphs: Xiaohongshu outfit posts build peer-to-peer trust through homophily and micro-influencer clusters, while Douyin live streams create high-density, real-time interaction hubs. Hashtags, lotteries, and IP collaborations act as diffusion triggers, strengthening weak ties, boosting engagement cascades, and expanding brand reach.

this post builds a UGC-driven diffusion loop: branded hashtags activate KOCs, triggering peer influence and community resonance among 18–35 users. Cross-platform listening then links hashtag “bursts” on Redbook/Douyin to downstream search and order spikes on Meituan/Ele.me.

This is a well-structured and thoughtful analytics plan. The key to success for Mister Softee lies in leveraging emotional nostalgia and real-time engagement. The focus on "reach" is a smart starting point, but the plan could be strengthened by emphasizing user-generated content. Since the target audience (18-35) loves sharing memories, actively encouraging and reposting customer photos (e.g., a contest for the best "truck selfie") would significantly boost authenticity and organic reach, turning casual viewers into brand advocates.

This blog brilliantly captures the shift from broad awareness to surgical data intelligence. By connecting Google Trends spikes with TikTok’s viral triggers, the focus on “Digital Snackers” is spot-on. The proposed dashboard is a powerful tool for translating real-time curiosity into actionable app engagement, effectively measuring the “why” behind the digital footprint.

This plan excels at audience precision. Targeting 18-26-year-olds in Hong Kong at 9:30 PM is a sharp strategy. The hypothesis that UGC and sustainability will outperform product shots is smart; for Gen Z, social media is about values and community, not just consumption. This data-driven pivot toward authentic storytelling is the right move.

This plan is spot-on for Jellycat. Prioritizing UGC volume and share rate over vanity metrics is perfect for a brand built on emotional connection. The "Fan of the Week" strategy transforms passive followers into active community advocates. By aligning with cultural trends like self-care and collectibility, the analysis correctly identifies that social success lies in participating in the conversation, not just broadcasting it.

This plan is a masterclass in strategic focus. Replacing gut instinct with a clear descriptive-to-prescriptive framework is exactly right. The "hybrid content" idea—merging popular tutorial formats with sustainability messaging—is a brilliant solution to the engagement gap. By prioritizing sentiment from X and hard data from Insights, this approach ensures the brand's values are communicated in a way the audience actually wants to receive.

This post is a well-structured, data-driven social media plan. It smartly moves beyond vanity metrics by focusing on actionable insights, specifically targeting how humor drives engagement to turn passive followers into active brand advocates for TamJai SamGor.

This post presents a logical, data-backed plan focused on reversing QQ's user decline. However, it relies on general industry reports and proposes tactical fixes (like hiding footprints) that feel minor compared to the existential threat from Douyin and WeChat.

This post offers a sophisticated, multi-platform analytics plan for iHerb, effectively integrating cultural nuance from Xiaohongshu with global metrics. By prioritizing "Save-to-Like" ratios and educational UGC over vanity metrics, it smartly aligns social strategy with genuine purchase intent and trust-building.

This post presents a clear, focused plan to boost McDonald's Instagram engagement among students. However, its recommendation to simply "post more" is superficial; the real challenge is creating culturally resonant content that competes with meme pages and peers in a young user's crowded feed.

This post presents an emotionally resonant, data-informed strategy for Tower Records' revival. By prioritizing topic volume and cultural "vibe" analysis over vanity metrics, it smartly positions physical stores as a counter-cultural movement. The challenge lies in converting this online noise into sustainable foot traffic.

This is a spot-on take!  You've nailed why Heytea is winning the "vibes" game while everyone else is just bleeding cash in price wars. Most brands are obsessed with "What's in the cup?", but you've realized Heytea is asking "What can you do with the cup?". Using UGC as the "gold standard" is a smart move because it's much harder to fake than a simple like. You've basically described a brand that's acting like your creative, meme-loving best friend. Solid stuff.

Great breakdown! You hit the nail on the head regarding Content Analysis. McDonald's is the king of 'standardized yet creative' content, and tracking what actually triggers a response is the only way to stay ahead. Totally agree on the 8 PM posting window—that's prime scrolling time for students and tired workers. Looking forward to those charts!

This is a textbook-level analysis of the Gen Z brand! Rhode really nails that "effortless trendy vibe". I noticed that each time they introduce new flavors, the interaction is simply explosive. Exactly as you said, Engagement Rate is the most crucial metric for evaluating this kind of social symbol brand.

Excellent analysis plan! It's interesting to define Saizeriya as a "social symbol". Indeed, when people go to Saizeriya, it's not just for food, but more of a social habit. You mentioned comparing the data between Pizza Hut and Genki Sushi. This competitive dimension is well chosen.

Solid strategy! Focusing on Reach as the primary KPI is a smart move for Nike—at this scale, it's all about staying top-of-mind and conquering the recommendation algorithm. I'm particularly interested in your 'Trend Analysis' approach; identifying that 'burst-and-decay' pattern is a game-changer for optimizing posting cadence.

Great analysis! I love that you're bridging the gap between a traditional 'Dai Pai Dong' and the modern Gen Z digital lifestyle. Focusing on Xiaohongshu and IG is a game-changer for a local brand like Hop Fat. The strategy of turning 'word-of-mouth' into 'digital storytelling' is spot on. Can’t wait to see how the 'Wok Hei' translates into a high Engagement Rate!

I particularly like your emphasis on Content Analysis. Since Subway's appeal lies in customization and value, identifying whether "behind-the-scenes prep" or "creator taste tests" drives more action will prevent wasted ad spend. Leveraging TikTok and Instagram Reels for "short, authentic food videos" perfectly aligns with how Gen Z and Millennials consume food content today.

This is a sharp, culturally nuanced proposal. By prioritizing Sentiment Score over raw volume, you’ve hit the core of NetEase’s "emotional moat." Using NLP to distinguish between nostalgic brand love and functional frustration is a brilliant strategy for maintaining Gen Z loyalty in a competitive, copyright-heavy streaming market.

This is a killer breakdown of how the "Snow King" is basically taking over the world! You nailed it by picking Engagement Rate as your KPI—for a brand as viral as Mixue, if people aren't meme-ing the mascot or huming the theme song, the magic is gone. Your plan to double down on UGC and micro-influencers is a total pro move; it keeps the brand feeling "cheap and fun" rather than just "cheap."

You’ve perfectly diagnosed the "love the food, fear the bill" tension among students. By leveraging Xiaohongshu and Instagram for a "200 HKD Budget Challenge,"  Haidilao is turning a price barrier into a fun, viral game. Focusing on "saves" and "shares" is the right move for Gen Z. It’s practical, data-driven, and honestly, exactly what Haidilao needs to win back the campus crowd!

Your strategy is solid! Focusing on Content Analysis for the 25–45 professional crowd on Facebook hits the mark. Shifting from static posts to functional, lifestyle-driven Reels is a smart move to capture workday attention. It’s a very practical, data-led approach!

Your strategy for Hot Wheels is super sleek! Using InsTrack to dissect engagement across parents, teens, and collectors is a pro move. Shifting toward Content Analysis to see if "Car Culture" reels outpace static "Toy" posts will really help redline their growth. It's a high-octane, data-driven plan!

This plan is a total vibe! Targeting mainland students and pros in HK via Xiaohongshu is a pro move—that’s exactly where they live. Focusing on "Saves" as a KPI is smart because for bubble tea, a save usually means a future sale. If your "Newcomer Guide" goes viral, the queues will be massive!

This UNIQLO analysis is incredibly thorough! You’ve perfectly captured how they bridge the gap between "basic wear" and "trendy lifestyle" across Xiaohongshu and Douyin. Focusing on UGC outfit challenges and live-stream interactivity is a winning formula for the 20-35 demographic. It’s a smart, 360-degree approach that turns simple clothes into social currency!

This POP MART strategy is incredibly sharp! You’ve hit the bullseye by focusing on the secondary market—that’s exactly where the brand’s reputation lives or dies. Using Trend Analysis to map sentiment spikes against product drops is a pro move to outsmart scalper drama. It’s a high-stakes, data-backed plan to keep the fan community loyal!

Good use of competitor analysis to guide action. I like that you mentioned Domino’s as a benchmark and then proposed interactive campaigns and better community management. One suggestion: clarify platform-specific tactics since TikTok and Facebook behave differently. Adding a posting cadence and testing plan would make this even more actionable.

Your McDonald’s China plan is clear and audience-focused. Targeting young parents and measuring positive comment rate on Meituan/Dianping, Douyin, and Xiaohongshu makes sense because it captures real dining feedback. I also like the 12‑month sentiment and keyword trend analysis and the competitor comparison with KFC and Burger King. To strengthen the method, explain how you’ll identify comments from the 25–45 segment and reduce noise from spam or incentivized reviews.

I like how your analysis links directly to strategy: if UGC outperforms studio shots, shifting budget to KOCs is a logical, data-driven recommendation. The target audience definition is accurate for Fenty. To improve, consider expanding data collection beyond Instagram or at least noting limitations, since TikTok and YouTube also drive beauty discovery.

Your McDonald’s plan is clear and practical. The goal and audience match the brand’s current traffic channels, and using interaction rate as the KPI is appropriate. I also like your data mix—platform insights plus Google Trends helps connect content performance with broader topic interest. To make the analysis stronger, define exactly how you’ll calculate interaction rate and consider adding video completion rate as a secondary KPI for TikTok.

Clear goal, KPI, and audience definition. Data sources are appropriate and the content-analysis approach fits the platform context. To improve, specify the time frame, sampling method, and how interaction rate is calculated. Add measurable targets and clearer competitor benchmarking.

Your blog sets up a clear, practical plan for analyzing Spotify’s social media performance. I like how you connect Spotify Wrapped and personalized playlists to the KPI of engagement—those examples make the goal feel realistic. The target audience definition is also appropriate and matches how music trends spread on TikTok and Instagram. One suggestion: consider defining engagement more precisely per platform (e.g., shares/retweets might matter more than saves on X). It could also help to mention a time frame for data collection and how you’ll compare content types fairly across different algorithms.

Your proposal focuses well on a measurable outcome: improving Facebook engagement for Starbucks HK. The examples of content types—drink photos, seasonal campaigns, store aesthetics—show you understand the brand’s current strategy. I think the comparison between lifestyle-oriented Reels and static product images could reveal useful insights. One suggestion is to include saves or link clicks if available, since “likes” alone can be a shallow metric. Also, when you say seasonal campaigns might underperform, it would be interesting to test whether it’s the theme or the execution that drives lower engagement. Overall, the reporting plan sounds professional.

This is well-structured and feels like a realistic analytics plan. The connection between product launches, updates, and shifts in sentiment is especially interesting—software releases can create excitement or frustration, so measuring before or after changes makes sense. I also like your action idea of filling the “off-season” with community-driven challenges like #ShotOniPhone-style campaigns. To strengthen the study, consider splitting sentiment by audience segment or topic (battery, pricing, iOS bugs) so you can see what is driving negativity. Adding a baseline period and comparison to competitors could make insights even sharper.

Your POP MART HK plan feels very “on brand” because it’s built around drop culture and turning hype into action. I like that you keep to one KPI (engagement rate on launch posts) and still connect outcomes using UTMs and Google Analytics—this makes the plan more than just vanity metrics. The 12‑week window and the fields you’ll capture are also really practical for content analysis. One suggestion: clarify how you’ll treat “lottery/restock” posts vs. “new drop” posts, since urgency and logistics may naturally drive different engagement patterns.

Your Sanrio plan is well-organized and the brand choice makes a lot of sense because character IP naturally performs well on social platforms. I like that you define awareness through engagement rate, since active interactions show real interest beyond impressions. The target audience is also clear, and including platforms like TikTok and Rednote fits Gen Z behavior. Your data sources are strong—combining native insights with Google Trends and questionnaires can explain why certain characters or collaborations spike. One suggestion: engagement rate can vary by platform, so define a consistent formula and report platform-specific benchmarks.

This blog effectively balances quantitative metrics with qualitative comment sampling for a comprehensive analysis approach. The content categorization aligning with business objectives demonstrates strong strategic thinking. The reporting structure with performance tables, visual charts, and actionable checklists is stakeholder-friendly. Expanding the competitive analysis to include other regional theme parks such as Shanghai Disneyland or Ocean Park could provide valuable benchmarking context for evaluating content performance.

This blog effectively connects Vivienne Westwood's brand heritage with contemporary social media strategy. The target audience definition balancing punk culture appreciation with sustainable consumption values is well-considered. The reporting structure with executive summary, visualizations, and actionable recommendations follows professional standards. Including competitor analysis from luxury brands with similar sustainability positioning could provide useful context for evaluating the proposed strategies and setting realistic KPI targets.

This blog presents a focused and practical approach to monitoring POP MART's secondary market-related discussions. The clear connection between data collection, trend analysis, and targeted actions demonstrates logical research design. The one-page reporting format with immediate, short-term, and long-term recommendations is stakeholder-friendly. Expanding the scope to include sentiment comparison across different product lines or collaboration series could reveal more nuanced patterns in consumer reactions.

This blog effectively combines strategic planning with practical execution details for HEYTEA's social media marketing. The clear reporting structure with executive summaries, dashboards, and action plans demonstrates professional communication awareness. The emphasis on UGC and KOC collaboration aligns well with current marketing trends. Including benchmark data from competitors or industry standards would further validate the proposed KPI targets and enhance the study's credibility.

This blog effectively outlines a social media analytics framework for Starbucks with clear objectives and audience segmentation. The emphasis on blending quantitative and qualitative data shows methodological awareness. The strategy section provides practical recommendations aligned with current marketing trends. To enhance the study further, incorporating competitor analysis or specific performance metrics would provide additional strategic context.

I find that this plan gives a clear, coherent framework for analyzing Rhode’s social media performance, especially by linking the brand’s “minimalist skincare” positioning and Gen Z “lifestyle symbol” ambition to a single, behavior-focused KPI: engagement rate. I like how the audience profile, data sources, and the choice of content analysis connect logically to the idea that “content is the product.” The proposed direction—replicating high-interaction content, adjusting distribution ratios, and adapting strategies by platform—also fits the goal of content-driven dissemination. Overall, it reads actionable and stakeholder-friendly, particularly with the modular reporting approach.

I think this analytics plan offers a clear roadmap for helping Game Science shift from a single breakout success to a sustainable franchise builder. It clearly defines the goal of franchise continuity and matches it with a focused KPI—cross-pollination rate—to test whether existing Wukong followers engage with “Zhongkui” teaser content. I also like the data mix of social listening for developer-level sentiment and audience retention on YouTube/Bilibili to measure interest beyond Wukong-specific updates. The trend analysis logic is easy to follow, and the “Universe Building” strategy, supported by developer diaries, aligns well with the idea of strengthening the studio identity. The reporting via a sentiment correlation matrix also feels decision-friendly.

I think that this Haidilao Hong Kong plan feels very practical and easy to roll out. Everything links back nicely to its biggest strength, which is the highly shareable service experience. The goal of this blog is clearly defined from awareness to actual visits, and that measurement stays focused with a simple weekly Instagram engagement target of 20 percent growth over eight weeks. The data setup also makes sense. Using Instagram Insights to track performance, GA4 with UTMs to read booking link signals, and hashtag and location-based UGC for qualitative insight feels well thought through. The 60-day content analysis themes are on point, and the follow-up actions feel realistic, like doubling down on service moment reels, running a lightweight UGC challenge, and easing queue frustration with clearer peak times and reservation messaging. The monthly reporting format should also work well for stakeholders.

I enjoyed reading this blog because it presents Starbucks’ social media analytics plan in a clear, step-by-step way, and I can easily see how each part supports the engagement-focused objective. The section on collecting both quantitative metrics and qualitative comments feels practical, and the idea of removing spam and organizing comments into themes makes the later comparison across content categories more consistent. Overall, I feel the plan is actionable and well-structured, and the report with charts would make insights easy to apply.

I enjoyed reading this blog because it explains POP MART Hong Kong’s social media analytics plan in a clear and practical way. I like that the goal is not only to build local awareness, but also to guide people from hype to action like e store purchases and event attendance. Focusing on one KPI, engagement rate on launch posts, makes the plan easy to track across Instagram and Facebook, with X used for wider reach. I also find the data collection list helpful, especially the 12 week window and the use of UTM links to connect content to outcomes. The content analysis and simple action steps feel realistic and useful.

Thank you for your valuable feedback. The selection of Hama Sushi and Sushi Express was not based on the assumption that they have the same market share or social media performance as Sushiro. Instead, they were chosen because they share similar business models, price ranges, service models, and target audiences (Young people who follow popular restaurant brands on social media). In this study, I aimed to minimize differences in product categories and dining environments so that changes in engagement rates could more clearly reflect differences in social media content strategies, posting frequency, and audience engagement, rather than being influenced by structural factors such as brand history or overall market size. Therefore, they are considered direct competitors with similar positioning. Their selection was based on methodological comparability, not an assumption that they are at the same level in overall performance.
Genki Sushi has higher brand awareness and a longer history in Hong Kong, and its inclusion in the study does provide a different benchmark (although, based on my dining experience, it did not show a significant difference compared to Sushiro and Hama Sushi). Compared to Hama Sushi and Sushi Express, Genki Sushi is more suitable as a higher-level or more attractive benchmark brand, allowing us to observe how a brand with stronger brand equity and market influence utilizes social media.
However, placing stronger brands on the same comparative tier can reduce the clarity of the explanation. This means that differences in engagement rates may depend not only on content strategy but also on brand loyalty, historical influence, or network size (as I mentioned in the first paragraph).
Thank you for your comment. It helps refine the analytical framework and enhances the overall depth and credibility of the research.

The focus on Snow King IP is smart—it's Mixue's core differentiator from competitors like Luckin who rely on celebrity endorsements. Using engagement rate instead of vanity metrics is the right call. However, I'm wondering: does high engagement actually translate to purchases? Mixue's viral nature sometimes creates "meme-only" buzz without real sales impact. Consider adding sentiment analysis to distinguish between fun engagement and genuine purchase intent. Also, the 15-35 demographic is quite broad—splitting into students vs. young professionals might give sharper insights for targeting.

This is a well-structured proposal. The focus on Instagram and TikTok makes sense for a lifestyle brand targeting 18-35 year olds. Using engagement rate as KPI is practical, though I'd suggest clarifying whether you're measuring engagement rate per post or per follower—this matters for interpretation. The content analysis approach works well for comparing seasonal promotions vs. everyday content. One thing: you might want to add a note about how you'll handle spam or bot activity in the data cleaning phase, since that can skew engagement metrics significantly.

The "cross-pollination rate" is a creative KPI—measuring whether Wukong fans follow to Zhongkui is genuinely smart for franchise building. However, I'm slightly concerned about data isolation. YouTube and Bilibili retention data is useful, but how do you distinguish Wukong loyalists from people just interested in the folklore? Someone watching a Zhongkui teaser might not be a Wukong fan at all. Also, the "universe building" strategy could backfire—if Zhongkui underperforms, it might drag down the Wukong brand. Sometimes keeping some distance between titles is safer for risk management.

The competitor analysis approach is useful in a crowded pizza market. Focusing on engagement rate and sentiment score gives you both quantity and quality of engagement. However, there's a technical issue: you mentioned video completion rate and website traffic from Google Analytics, but these are private metrics for competitors like Domino's. How do you plan to benchmark against them? You'll likely need to rely on public engagement metrics or third-party estimation tools. Also, your action strategies (UGC challenges, IP collaborations) are solid, but consider platform-specific tactics—TikTok and Facebook work very differently.

Your clear goal of increasing Facebook engagement with a specific KPI is practical. Targeting 25-45 professionals in Hong Kong makes sense for café culture. The content analysis approach works well for comparing Reels vs static images and seasonal vs everyday content. One suggestion: consider adding Instagram to your platform mix since your target demographic is also very active there. Also, for the seasonal vs everyday comparison, it might be worth distinguishing between localized seasonal content (like mooncake lattes, egg tart frappuccinos for Hong Kong) versus global seasonal content (Christmas, Valentine's)—they might resonate very differently with HK audiences.

Genshin Impact is a smart choice—it's a globally successful game with strong community engagement. Targeting Bilibili for domestic fans and Twitter/X for global audience covers the key bases. I'm curious about the "dry spells" between updates though—this is a real challenge for live-service games. Have you considered adding social listening to catch community frustrations early before they spiral? Also, your content categories (lore-heavy, combat-focused, community-driven) are good, but how do you actually quantify "emotional response"? That seems difficult to measure consistently. The "deep-dive" content strategy is solid, but I'd like to see more concrete metrics for success.

Good focus on the "new Chinese vintage girly" style—it's a distinctive positioning that sets SHUSHU/TONG apart from mainstream fast fashion. Using engagement rate as KPI is practical, but I'm wondering: since you're targeting young women who are highly visual content consumers, would saves/bookmarks be a better indicator of genuine interest? Likes can be casual, but saves often indicate actual consideration. Also, comparing Xiaohongshu, Instagram, and Weibo simultaneously is ambitious—these platforms have very different user behaviors and algorithms. Maybe prioritize one platform first to get meaningful insights rather than spreading thin across three.

Focusing on young parents (25-45) and using Meituan, Dianping, Douyin, and Xiaohongshu shows good market understanding—these are exactly where Chinese families make dining decisions. The positive comment rate as KPI is practical for reputation management. However, 12 months is a long time for trend analysis, and seasonal effects might skew the data. How will you filter out incentivized reviews (reviews given for discounts/free food)? Those can inflate positive rates artificially. Have you considered using Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis to break down sentiment by specific attributes like "food safety," "service speed," and "playground cleanliness"? That would give McDonald's more actionable operational insights.

The "Portuguese tart moment" hook is relatable and makes the proposal engaging. Using CTR as the main KPI is much more actionable than vanity metrics—it directly measures the scroll-to-order journey. The multi-platform data collection (Instagram, Facebook, YouTube + Google Analytics) gives a complete view. I appreciate the stakeholder-specific reporting approach—different teams really do need different levels of detail. One thing I'd suggest: have you considered conversion rate beyond CTR? Clicks don't always equal orders. Also, for the A/B testing, try isolating variables—test format first, then caption style, then posting time. Testing everything at once makes it hard to know what actually moved the needle.

Good analysis plan for VALORANT. Segmenting the audience into casual gamers (TikTok/Instagram) and competitive enthusiasts (YouTube/VCT) makes sense. The specific metrics you mentioned—Twitch view counts, TikTok completion rates, UGC submissions—are relevant to gaming content. However, I'm curious about how you'll get 1,000 UGC samples and ensure they're not bot-generated? The anomaly detection method you mentioned is a good start. Also, the 78% positive sentiment is interesting, but what about the 22% negative? If complaints center on technical issues, that's valuable feedback for the dev team. The proposal mentions platform-specific UGC challenges but doesn't specify how you'll measure which platform delivers the best ROI.

Great choice focusing on a toy brand with such a strong collector community! Your engagement rate KPI is practical, and the three audience segments (parents, teenagers, car enthusiasts) give a clear picture of who you're trying to reach. The InsTrack tool seems like a solid choice for data collection.

One suggestion: consider adding "saves" to your engagement metrics since收藏 often indicates genuine interest, especially for model car enthusiasts who might want to reference products later. Also, specifying the time period for data collection (e.g., last 3 months) would make your analysis more concrete.

Love the "Portuguese tart moment" vibe here—just like KFC, you're focusing on the actual conversion journey rather than just likes. The CTR KPI is much more actionable for lead generation. Your hypothesis about ASMR vs workplace comedy is interesting and well-reasoned.

One thing to consider: how will you track the "View-to-Click" path specifically? TikTok's analytics might need some UTM setup to properly attribute downloads. Also, it might help to clarify which content type (ASMR or comedy) actually drives more views first, since CTR depends on having enough impressions to measure.

Your +20% target in 8 weeks is ambitious and gives clear direction! The focus on Instagram makes sense for visual-heavy content like service moments and food videos. I like how you're combining UGC from hashtags with quantitative metrics—that should give a fuller picture.

The mention of GA4 with UTM tags is smart for tracking the full journey from social to bookings. One thing to think about: how will you isolate Haidilao's organic content performance from paid promotions? Mixing them might skew the engagement rate. Also, adding sentiment analysis to the comments could reveal more nuanced insights beyond just engagement numbers.

Smart comparison between IP collaborations and standard product launches! This is a real strategic question for HEYTEA since IP partnerships are expensive. The save rate as a proxy for purchase intent is a clever idea—it makes sense that people save posts when they're actually planning to buy.

Your hypothesis about standard posts potentially having better long-term retention is worth testing. One thing to add: it might help to define what counts as "IP collaboration" vs "standard product" content, since some seasonal launches might blur the line. Also, considering the time period for comparison (e.g., are IP campaigns usually around specific holidays?) would make the analysis more rigorous.

Competitor analysis is a great choice for a brand in such a crowded space! Comparing with Hama Sushi and Sushi Express makes sense since they have similar target audiences. Using both Instagram and Xiaohongshu covers both international and Chinese market perspectives, which is important for a Japanese brand.

The idea of analyzing user-generated content to capture "how consumers naturally discuss the brand" is solid—it goes beyond just looking at official posts. One thing to consider: engagement rate calculations should be consistent across platforms since algorithms work differently. Also, it might help to specify which metrics matter most for each platform (e.g., 收藏 rate might be more meaningful on Xiaohongshu).

This is a clear, well-structured analytics plan with a strong link between *content strategy* (“de-commercialized” worker-perspective storytelling) and the *intended outcome* (brand trust). The audience definition and reasoning for using sentiment analysis are also convincing, and the crisis-monitoring angle makes the proposal feel practical rather than purely academic.

That said, the KPI needs tightening: “Net Sentiment Score” should be precisely defined (e.g., weighting neutral, handling sarcasm, bots, and repeated commenters) and ideally paired with at least one behavioral trust proxy (e.g., “save/share rate,” repeat viewing, or comment depth) to avoid over-relying on sentiment alone. The methodology section could also acknowledge sampling bias and platform constraints—scraping from Douyin and hashtag discussions can skew toward more vocal users.

One more improvement: the action thresholds are currently vague (“certain threshold”). If you set a baseline (pre-campaign sentiment) and define a measurable lift target, your strategy recommendations will read much more credible and testable.

This is a solid, easy-to-follow framework, and it matches your goal well: you define the audience clearly, pick relevant platforms, and choose a method (content analysis) that fits visual-first channels. The strategy section is also practical—UGC, hashtags, and short-video pacing are all realistic levers for boosting interaction.

To make it stronger, tighten the KPI definition. “Interaction rate” can vary by platform (e.g., interactions ÷ impressions vs. ÷ reach vs. ÷ views), so you should specify the exact formula for Instagram and TikTok, and decide whether you’ll weight shares/saves more heavily than likes (since they often reflect deeper engagement).

The analysis part could also be more operational: explain your coding scheme (what variables you’ll tag—theme, format, tone, visual style, CTA, posting time), and how you’ll ensure consistency (e.g., a simple codebook or double-coding a sample). Also note that Google Trends measures *search interest*, not social engagement, so frame it as a supporting signal for broader attention rather than a direct KPI driver.

Overall: clear plan, but it will read more “research-ready” if you add precise KPI formulas, a simple content-coding rubric, and one or two outcome metrics beyond engagement (e.g., brand mention volume, sentiment, or follower growth) to show impact, not just interaction.

Apple stands out as a brand that masterfully blends innovation, design, and user experience. Known for its sleek hardware, intuitive software, and tightly integrated ecosystem, Apple consistently delivers products that prioritize simplicity and performance. From the iPhone to the MacBook and beyond, each device reflects a commitment to quality and attention to detail that has cultivated a loyal global following. While often positioned at a premium price point, Apple’s offerings justify their cost through longevity, seamless updates, and a cohesive experience across devices—making it not just a tech company, but a lifestyle choice for millions.

Saizeriya is a Japanese casual dining chain that has mastered the art of affordable Italian cuisine without compromising on consistency or accessibility. Known for its remarkably low prices, generous portions, and family-friendly atmosphere, Saizeriya offers staples like pasta, pizza, risotto, and grilled dishes that cater to everyday diners rather than fine-dining enthusiasts. While it may not deliver authentic Italian flavors, its menu is thoughtfully adapted to local palates—especially across East Asia—striking a balance between familiarity and culinary novelty.

With a highly efficient operational model, standardized kitchen processes, and a no-frills approach, Saizeriya keeps costs down while maintaining reliable quality. It’s a go-to choice for students, families, and budget-conscious food lovers seeking a satisfying meal in a relaxed setting. In a world where dining out can quickly become expensive, Saizeriya stands out as a rare example of value-driven hospitality done right.

Luckin Coffee is a rapidly growing Chinese coffee brand that has redefined the local coffee market with its focus on affordability, digital convenience, and extensive store coverage. By leveraging a mobile-first ordering system, self-pickup, and delivery services, Luckin has successfully made coffee a daily habit for millions of consumers, challenging the traditional notion that quality coffee must be expensive and infrequent. The brand frequently launches innovative and viral products—such as its famous “Moutai-flavored latte”—demonstrating sharp marketing instincts and strong appeal to younger demographics.

After overcoming an early financial scandal, Luckin underwent a successful restructuring, returned to profitability, and has since become China’s largest coffee chain by number of stores, with growing global ambitions. While it may not match specialty cafés in terms of premium ambiance or artisanal experience, Luckin excels in accessibility, value, speed, and product innovation—making it a standout example of tech-driven, efficient, and locally adapted new retail in the modern consumer landscape.

From a social networking perspective, this project presents a clear objective and a logical structure, particularly in its focus on engagement rate as a key performance indicator and its attention to young consumers on platforms such as TikTok, Instagram, and Xiaohongshu. These choices are appropriate because social networking platforms rely heavily on user interaction, and engagement can reflect how well content resonates with online audiences. The use of content analysis to categorize posts (e.g., memes, mascots, promotions, and user-generated content) is also suitable for understanding which types of content perform better in social media environments. However, the analysis mainly focuses on marketing performance rather than deeper social networking dynamics. For example, the project does not examine how content spreads through user networks, such as sharing behavior, reposting patterns, or interactions among users in the comment sections. Additionally, relying mainly on engagement rate may oversimplify the measurement of brand awareness, as high engagement does not necessarily translate into long-term brand recognition or loyalty. Incorporating network-based indicators, such as diffusion patterns, community interaction, or sentiment analysis of comments, could provide a more comprehensive understanding of how Mixue’s content circulates and influences users within social networks. Overall, while the project provides a solid starting point, integrating more social networking concepts would strengthen the analytical depth of the study.

This proposal presents a clear and well-organized plan for analyzing Nike’s social media performance. The objective of increasing brand awareness is clearly defined, and the use of engagement rate as the main KPI is appropriate because interactions such as likes, comments, and shares reflect how actively users engage with content on social media platforms. The identification of young adults as the target audience and the emphasis on visually appealing and motivational content also align well with Nike’s brand identity and the typical preferences of users on platforms like Instagram.

However, the analysis mainly focuses on basic engagement metrics and content categories, which makes the approach somewhat limited from a social networking perspective. While the proposal examines which types of posts generate higher engagement, it does not fully consider how content spreads within social networks. For example, it does not explore reposting behavior, interactions among users in the comment sections, or the role of online communities in amplifying brand messages. In addition, relying only on engagement rate may not fully capture the level of brand awareness or the long-term influence of Nike’s social media presence.

To improve the analysis, the study could include additional aspects related to social networking dynamics. For instance, it could examine how users share content within their networks, analyze the sentiment of comments, or investigate how user-generated content contributes to wider content diffusion. Including these elements would provide a deeper understanding of how Nike’s content circulates and influences audiences within social media networks.

The proposal outlines a straightforward plan to evaluate ZA Bank’s social media performance and improve its brand awareness among young digital users. The focus on Gen Z and Millennials is reasonable, as these groups are more open to virtual banking services and are highly active on social media platforms. Using data from Instagram, Facebook, and Google Analytics is also helpful because it allows the study to observe both social media activity and website traffic.

However, the analysis mainly focuses on reach and competitor comparison, which makes the approach somewhat limited from a social networking perspective. While reach can show how widely the content is viewed, it does not fully explain how users interact with the content or how information spreads across networks. For instance, the proposal does not consider sharing behavior, user discussions in the comment sections, or interactions between users, all of which are important features of social networks.

To strengthen the study, it could include additional indicators such as engagement rate or sentiment analysis of user comments. Examining how users react to posts or discuss ZA Bank online could provide deeper insights into how the brand spreads and gains influence within social media networks. Overall, the proposal provides a good starting point, but incorporating more elements related to social interaction would make the analysis more comprehensive.

This proposal provides a structured plan to analyze Starbucks’ social media performance, with a clear focus on increasing brand awareness among young adults. Choosing reach as the main KPI is understandable because it reflects how widely Starbucks’ content is exposed to potential customers on platforms like Instagram. The identification of young adults as the target audience is also appropriate, since Starbucks’ brand is closely associated with lifestyle, urban culture, and visually appealing content that performs well on social media.

However, the proposal focuses heavily on reach and competitor comparison, which may not fully capture how audiences actually interact with Starbucks’ content. From a social networking perspective, brand influence on social media often depends not only on how many people see the content but also on how users engage with it and share it within their networks. The current plan does not discuss user interaction in comments, reposting behavior, or how lifestyle-related content might encourage community participation.

To strengthen the analysis, it could include additional measures such as engagement rate or sentiment analysis to understand how audiences respond to Starbucks’ posts. It might also be useful to consider how influencer collaborations or user-generated content contribute to social visibility through peer sharing. By including these aspects, the study could provide a deeper understanding of how Starbucks’ content spreads and shapes brand perception within social media communities.

This proposal takes a slightly different approach compared with typical social media analytics plans by focusing on **brand loyalty and sentiment** rather than only visibility or engagement. The use of **Net Sentiment Score** as the main KPI is interesting because it reflects how users feel about Spotify’s recommendation system and product updates, which is very relevant for a platform that relies heavily on algorithms to personalize user experience. The plan also connects well with Spotify’s audience—Gen Z and Millennials who use music streaming not only for entertainment but also for identity expression and social sharing.

However, from a social networking perspective, the analysis could still go further. The proposal mainly focuses on sentiment trends over time, but it does not fully explore how conversations spread across social networks. For example, it does not consider which users or communities drive the discussions, how posts are shared or amplified, or whether certain influencers or fan communities play a role in shaping public opinion. These network dynamics are important because music platforms like Spotify often rely on social sharing and community-driven trends.

To strengthen the study, it could include elements such as identifying key discussion hubs, analyzing how viral posts or memes influence sentiment, or examining how user-generated playlists and Spotify Wrapped posts circulate within social networks. Adding these aspects would provide a more complete picture of how user sentiment forms and spreads online, making the analysis more closely aligned with social networking concepts.

One thing I really like about this plan is that it highlights the importance of localised content, like using Cantonese slogans. It shows an understanding that a global brand needs to connect with local audiences in a more personal way, which I think is a smart touch. However, I did notice a possible gap between the target audience and the data sources. The plan identifies young adults aged 18–30 as the main target group, which of course includes the large and important market of mainland Chinese youth. But it only suggests using Instagram and Google Analytics for data collection. Since these platforms aren't accessible in mainland China, the data might not fully capture how this significant portion of the audience actually engages with the brand. Including local platforms like Weibo or Douyin could give a much clearer picture.

One strength of this plan is its choice of Click-Through Rate (CTR) as the primary KPI. Moving beyond vanity metrics like likes and comments, CTR reflects a shift from brand awareness to sales orientation, which aligns well with Zara's business goals. However, the plan remains somewhat vague on how this KPI would actually be calculated and tracked. It mentions using Instagram Insights and Google Analytics, but does not explain how data from these separate platforms would be integrated. For instance, would CTR be calculated as clicks divided by impressions? If so, how would impression data from Instagram be matched with traffic data in Google Analytics? More clarity on the data collection process behind this metric would strengthen the proposal.

This plan stands out for its strong practicality. The goals are clearly defined, and the KPI selection effectively balances short-term conversions with long-term user value. The data sources are comprehensive and well-considered—combining Meta's native insights with third-party platforms like Qi Mai provides a more complete view of campaign performance. The strategy recommendations are actionable, and the reporting design using Looker Studio demonstrates an understanding of how different stakeholders will use the insights. One aspect that could be refined is the definition of conversion rate. The plan measures it through downloads and first orders, but breaking it down into two stages—"impression to click" and "click to first order"—would allow for more precise identification of where user drop-off occurs. This small adjustment would make the analysis even more targeted and actionable.

I really like how the platform selection reflects an understanding of different market habits, Bilibili for Chinese users, Twitter for global conversations, and YouTube for long-form content. It's a thoughtful touch. Building on that, I wonder if adding a comparative layer could make the analysis even stronger. The plan already tracks global hashtags and fan art communities, but seeing how those metrics compare to the broader ACG sector's averages—for example, whether Genshin's fan engagement is outpacing similar games—would help benchmark its actual market position.

Rather than a general engagement conversion rate, a metric such as "in-store participation conversion rate" would more directly measure how many users who engaged with trending topics actually visited a restaurant and made a purchase.

Overall, this social media analysis proposal for Luckin Coffee is well-structured, strictly adhering to the course’s five-step framework. Breaking it down: (1) Goal Setting& Audience: Selecting “enhancing brand awareness + measured by engagement rate” aligns well with Luckin’s operational logic of frequent new product launches and promotions. The target audience accurately captures the core demographic of 18–35 year-olds who are mobile-centric and favour collaborations and trending topics; (2) Data Collection: Utilising publicly available data from official accounts on Weibo, Douyin, and Xiaohongshu ensures realistic and accessible sources with clearly defined metrics; (3) Analysis & Interpretation: Employing content analysis with a dual focus on “interaction differences driven by collaborations” and “variations in content formats” is highly reasonable, with specific problem-setting facilitating conclusive outcomes; (4) Strategy & Action: Directly translating analysis into actionable directions – “optimising content formats, adjusting release timings, and prioritising high-engagement platforms” – demonstrates a closed-loop process from insight to decision-making; (5) Reporting: The dashboard + concise report format facilitates rapid stakeholder comprehension and implementation.

Suggestion: Further detail within Strategy (e.g., defining ‘collaboration effectiveness’ metrics, and outlining A/B testing or cohort comparison approaches) would enhance the plan’s “operability and verifiability”.

Really clear and practical plan—RED is a great choice for awareness because discovery is so driven by content quality and “real” UGC.

One suggestion to make the reach KPI more informative: consider splitting reach into “cold reach” vs. “warm reach.” On Xiaohongshu, a post reaching existing followers (warm) is very different from reaching new users through search/recommendation (cold), even if the total reach number looks the same. If you tag posts by format and track a simple set of companion metrics like new-follower rate per 1,000 views and save rate (often a stronger “intent” signal on RED than likes), you’ll be able to tell not just which content gets seen, but which content actually grows awareness among new audiences and sticks in people’s minds.

This is a strong, well-structured plan—Sanrio’s character-first approach and your focus on younger audiences fit the platform reality really well. It may also help to tag posts by specific characters and themes so you can see which IPs actually drive new followers and sustained engagement, not just one-off spikes.

Really solid plan, especially how you tie social performance to real outcomes like downloads and first orders instead of stopping at likes. One thing that could make your conversion KPI even clearer is separating the new-user path from the referral path, since download to first order and invitation code to first order can behave very differently even with the same discount message. If you can tag links by content type and check where people drop off, like lots of clicks but low coupon claiming, or coupons claimed but no checkout, it becomes much easier to fix the exact step. Also love the idea of putting the code and download prompt at the end of Reels, I’m curious whether UGC ends up bringing in higher-quality first orders than pure coupon posters.

Really nice, easy-to-follow plan, and the content examples feel very “Volkswagen HK” especially the Golf handling clips and ID.4 tech demos. It could also be interesting to compare reach by content angle, like performance driving, smart features, or real owner stories, and then see which one brings the biggest bump in profile visits or test drive enquiries. That would help you turn “more people saw it” into a clearer answer on what kind of awareness is actually moving people closer to considering an ID.4 or Golf.
