Focusing on reach as the main KPI is perfect for raising this underrated brand’s profile. Turning data into real strategies like mixing public welfare with IP collabs, forms a tight, clear logic to help it stand out and reach more young people. Great!

This plan centers on the brand’s core drop culture and the local audience’s love for scarcity and IP storytelling. Picking engagement rate on launch content as the core KPI hits the nail on the head for the brand’s HK operation needs, and distinguishing X as a reach channel while digging into Instagram/Facebook’s conversion value shows great platform insight. Practical strategy to boost local awareness and user action. It's an excellent example of knowledge building for trendy IP brand social media operations.

I like how you clearly defined the goal of increasing both engagement and conversions instead of just focusing on followers. Your use of engagement rate as the main KPI makes the plan feel practical and measurable. The combination of local culture and digital strategy is also very relevant for young diners in Hong Kong. Maybe you could add one or two example post ideas to make the strategy even more concrete. Your target audience analysis is very detailed and realistic, especially the point about young people being “time-poor” and relying on social media for food decisions. That insight really explains why platforms like Instagram and Xiaohongshu are important. I think this helps justify your strategy very well. It makes the whole plan feel grounded in real user behavior.

You started with your personal interest in Pop Mart — it makes the post more engaging and authentic to read. Your focus on reach and impressions fits really well with the goal of brand awareness, and the platforms you chose make sense for this age group. The content analysis idea is practical too, especially comparing collabs, drops, and videos. It might be interesting to also look at user-generated content like unboxing posts, since fans often create a lot of free exposure for the brand.

Your plan is very clear and structured, which makes it easy to follow each step from goals to reporting. I like that you focused on one specific KPI (engagement rate) instead of tracking too many metrics, since that keeps the analysis more actionable. Categorizing promotions by theme is also a smart way to see which food items actually attract attention. Maybe you could also consider linking engagement to real sales or store visits to show the business impact more directly.

The proposal feels very realistic and easy to apply in practice. The target audience is clearly defined, and linking Subway to sports, gaming, and high-protein meals makes the positioning very relatable for young consumers. Your content analysis plan is also detailed, especially labeling posts by category and format to compare performance. I think the idea of using customer-generated content is smart because it can boost both engagement and authenticity at the same time.

What makes your proposal particularly strong is how you connect social media activities with real business outcomes like app downloads and first orders, rather than focusing only on likes or views. This makes the analysis feel much more meaningful from a marketing perspective. I also think your combination of platform insights, app-side data, and sentiment analysis creates a well-rounded understanding of both user behavior and customer satisfaction. The way you group content into different themes and then match posting times with download trends is a smart approach to identifying what truly drives conversions. Using visuals and a Looker Studio dashboard should also help stakeholders quickly understand the results and take action. Overall, the plan feels detailed, data-driven, and very practical for a real campaign.

This blog post presents a clear, well-structured social media analysis plan for Nintendo that follows a complete framework: brand overview, goals, audience, data collection, analysis, strategy, and reporting.
Its strengths include a focused sentiment analysis goal, appropriate platform and audience selection, and practical, actionable social media strategies. The plan is logical and easy to understand, making it suitable as a basic analysis proposal.
However, the plan is somewhat basic and lacks depth. It relies on only one KPI (sentiment distribution) without additional metrics such as engagement or topic analysis. It also misses specific details like keyword lists, sample size, and data filtering methods. Adding competitive analysis and more detailed execution steps would further improve the plan’s professionalism and feasibility.

The social media analytics plan for Hong Kong Disneyland is well-structured, practical, and strongly targeted. It clearly defines goals focused on brand appeal, engagement, and conversion, with a specific and measurable KPI—engagement rate. The audience segmentation (families, young couples, Disney IP fans) is highly appropriate for the theme park industry. The data collection across Instagram and Rednote covers both international and local tourists effectively, and combining quantitative metrics with qualitative comment analysis adds valuable depth. Using content analysis to compare experience promotion and immersive storytelling is logical and helps identify high-performing content. The proposed strategies—optimizing content, encouraging UGC, and conducting A/B testing—are actionable and directly support business goals. The reporting section is detailed and stakeholder-friendly.
The plan could be improved by including competitor analysis, long-term performance tracking, and sentiment analysis to better understand audience emotions. Overall, it is a professional and complete social media analytics proposal.

Your proposal for McDonald’s is strategic, data-driven, and highly practical, with a sharp focus on leveraging digital metrics to boost brand awareness and bridge online curiosity to offline conversion. It stands out for its targeted audience definition (Digital Snackers aged 18–34), relevant, multi-source data collection (Google Trends, TikTok Creative Center, Instagram Insights), and a clear performance analysis approach that benchmarks against key fast-food competitors. The KPI (Google Trends Search Interest Index) is specific and tied directly to the core goal, while the "double down on the spike" strategy is actionable and aligns with real-time digital behavior—turning search peaks into app user conversions is a smart link between online metrics and business outcomes. The interactive dashboard reporting also ensures stakeholder clarity on trend-to-engagement correlations.
Minor gaps include a lack of details on how to segment regional search interest for localized strategies, and no mention of measuring post-conversion metrics (e.g., app order volume) to validate the impact of social/digital efforts. Additionally, it does not address potential risks (e.g., viral content backlash) or how to sustain baseline search interest between campaign peaks. Overall, it’s a strong, modern proposal tailored to McDonald’s digital-native audience and 2026’s social landscape.

The proposal provides a clear and practical social media analytics plan for CHICHA San Chen in the Hong Kong market. It has a focused goal, a well‑defined target audience, and uses a solid competitor analysis framework to compare performance with other bubble tea brands. The choice of engagement rate as the core KPI is suitable for visual and lifestyle‑focused content. The data source (Instagram Insights) is relevant and reliable for the target group.But the plan is relatively basic. It lacks specific content categorization, more detailed metrics such as sentiment or reach, and clear actionable strategies beyond general adjustment. Adding content themes, collaboration analysis, and regional preferences would strengthen the depth and feasibility of the study.

This blog proposes a bold and creative social media analytics plan for Tower Records to revive physical music culture. It has a clear mission, ambitious goals, and a strong focus on localization and global expansion. The plan effectively combines social media data, streaming trends, and regional music preferences to guide store openings. It also highlights meaningful KPIs like topic volume and uses data-driven strategies to attract real music fans. However, the plan lacks specific details on data collection methods, sample size, and risk evaluation. It also does not fully explain how to measure long-term business performance beyond social discussion. With more concrete metrics and implementation details, the plan would be more practical and persuasive.

Your plan highlights an interesting aspect of Starbucks’ strategy by focusing on engagement as the central objective rather than simply promoting products. For a lifestyle brand, building conversations and community interaction on platforms like Instagram and TikTok is often more valuable than direct advertising, so the KPI you selected makes sense. The combination of platform analytics and Google Analytics is also useful because it connects on-platform interaction with off-platform behavior. I also appreciate the effort to separate quantitative metrics from qualitative insights by examining user comments and reactions. One additional angle that might strengthen the study is comparing seasonal campaigns with everyday posts, since Starbucks is well known for limited-time drinks that often generate strong online discussion.

The proposal captures well how fast-moving the skincare industry is and why monitoring trends can be more useful than only looking at past engagement data. The focus on hashtags, ingredient discussions, and influencer activity reflects how many consumers actually learn about skincare products online today. I also found the practical strategy suggestions interesting, especially the idea of adjusting the content mix if educational posts outperform direct product promotion. That kind of insight could help the brand build credibility while still promoting its products. Another aspect that might be worth exploring is how user-generated reviews or routine videos influence trust, since many skincare buyers rely heavily on peer experiences before trying a new brand.

Your proposal takes an interesting perspective by focusing on brand reputation rather than only engagement or reach. Concentrating on young parents and their concerns about health, cleanliness, and family-friendly environments makes the objective very specific and relevant for the Chinese market. The choice of platforms such as Meituan, Dianping, Douyin, and Xiaohongshu also reflects where real customer feedback usually appears after dining experiences. Tracking keyword trends and sentiment over time should provide useful insight into how public perception changes during different campaigns or seasons. One possible extension could be examining how specific family-oriented promotions or children’s menu launches influence these sentiment shifts, since those initiatives might directly affect how parents evaluate the brand.

The blog is especially engaging because it combines personal observation with detailed analysis. The comparison between Chagee’s strong storytelling on Xiaohongshu and its more promotional Instagram presence highlights a clear gap in strategy, and that insight makes the entire proposal feel very convincing. I also found the competitor comparison effective—contrasting Starbucks’ more corporate approach with Silk Tea’s localized storytelling helps explain why engagement levels differ so much. Another strength is how you support your points with visuals, tables, and engagement benchmarks, which makes the argument easier to follow. The recommendations about cultural storytelling, local collaborations, and micro-influencers are particularly practical because they directly connect online engagement with real customer experiences in Hong Kong. Overall, the post reads less like a simple assignment and more like a thoughtful marketing case study.

This proposal provides a clear and logical framework for analyzing ZA Bank’s social media performance. Focusing on reach as the main KPI makes sense for a virtual bank that aims to attract new users and expand awareness among younger digital audiences. The choice to conduct competitor analysis is also appropriate, since brands like Mox and WeLab Bank operate in the same highly competitive fintech space. By comparing their content strategies, it should be easier to identify gaps or opportunities in ZA Bank’s messaging. I also found the idea of evaluating which financial features or promotions generate the most interaction particularly useful, because it connects social media activity with real customer interest in the product. Adding short-form educational videos as a potential strategy is a strong suggestion, since many young users prefer quick, easy-to-understand financial content.

This is a very thoughtful and well-structured proposal. I really like how you connect Bilibili’s pan-entertainment shift with a concrete, measurable analytics framework, especially the idea of using interaction depth to capture true user engagement.
This plan is very clear and easy to understand. I like how the content ideas connect naturally with POP MART’s drop culture and different platforms.

This plan reads very smoothly and feels quite realistic. The way you connect outfit content, collaborations, and live stream engagement across platforms makes a lot of sense, and I can easily see how the analysis could lead to those actions.

This is a very detailed and practical plan. The way goals, KPIs, and data sources are connected makes the whole analysis feel realistic, and the mix of content and competitor analysis fits McDonald’s fast campaign cycles well.

This proposal feels very confident and well argued. The connection between social engagement, search demand, and brand perception is clear, and the strategy section does a good job of turning those insights into concrete next steps for CASETiFY HK.

Your decision to use LLMs for sentiment labeling on Douyin comments and danmu is a highly advanced and practical approach. Measuring the Net Sentiment Score instead of just raw engagement metrics aligns perfectly with the goal of evaluating deep brand trust. This is a brilliant analytical framework!

The way this proposal frames engagement rate as a measure of 'aesthetic resonance' is highly original. Furthermore, benchmarking the performance against 'The Journey of a Landscape Painting' provides a very practical industry context for evaluating the data. This is a fantastic approach to modernizing traditional culture on social media!

Your idea to analyze local content, specifically Cantonese slogans, stands out as a brilliant strategy for regional marketing. Categorizing the posts this way provides a very clear path to understanding what truly connects with specific demographics in Hong Kong and Canton. Excellent work making the action steps so concrete!

Excellent post. Targeting Xiaohongshu and Instagram makes perfect sense for reaching college students. I really appreciate how your plan directly connects the sentiment analysis findings with a concrete influencer campaign to change their perception of the price.

Great proposal! Your breakdown of Sanrio's strategy is highly actionable. Categorizing the content analysis into character-focused posts versus product collaborations is a very smart way to figure out what truly drives audience engagement.

Great analysis! I totally agree that they’re underestimated compared to brands like Mixue. Your idea of blending their existing public welfare image with trendier IP collabs is a smart way to keep the brand’s soul while actually getting noticed on Rednote.

Really enjoyed reading this! As a regular drinker myself, I’ve always felt that Chagee’s HK Instagram was missing that 'vibe' they have on Rednote. Your plan to use Tableau for tracking the correlation between engagement and foot traffic is a very professional touch—it moves the conversation from just 'making pretty posts' to actual business impact.

This is such a relevant topic! You’ve really captured the ‘love-hate’ relationship fans have with POP MART’s secondary market. I think focusing on ‘fairness’ and ‘authenticity’ as key sentiment triggers is spot on—for Gen Z collectors, the perceived fairness of the drop is often more important than the product itself. Great job identifying that friction point!

Solid analysis! I really like how you narrowed the focus down to the ID.4 and Golf models—it makes the plan feel much more concrete. This is a clear and practical social media analytics plan for Starbucks, aligned well with its lifestyle brand positioning. Focusing on customer engagement (rather than just product promotion) as the primary goal is strategic, and using engagement rate as the core KPI effectively measures content resonance with the 18–35 target audience—who are highly active on Instagram and TikTok. Collecting both quantitative (engagement metrics) and qualitative (user comments) data from multiple platforms, plus cleaning data to remove spam/irrelevant posts, ensures analysis accuracy. Content analysis is a fitting method for Starbucks’ diverse content formats, helping identify high-performing types to guide strategy.

This is a practical social media analytics plan for POP MART, directly addressing its key pain point: negative public opinion from the secondary market. Focusing on sentiment metrics as the core KPI. The target audience—18-30 collectors and casual buyers, especially those valuing fairness, pricing and authenticity—is accurately defined, covering both loyal fans and potential customers at risk of leaving due to controversies. Collecting data from Weibo, Xiaohongshu and Douyin—platforms where POP MART discussions are most concentrated—and capturing key details like high-engagement comments and relevant keywords ensures comprehensive insight into public opinion.

This blog clearly explains the logic behind Meixue Bingcheng's journey from a viral hit to long-term operation, forming a complete closed loop from goals, data to strategies. The idea of using IP content instead of simple advertising promotion is very inspiring and has great reference value for brand social media analysis.

This blog provides a profound analysis of B Station's transition from ACG to a broader form of entertainment. It accurately captures the core contradiction of the platform's transformation. By using data to measure content penetration and user retention, it offers significant reference value for brands entering B Station.

This social media analysis of Haidilao is very practical. It accurately captures the hidden eating methods, IP collaborations, and service experiences that young people like. By using data to convert online popularity into in-store visits, the approach is clear and has practical reference value.

Keeta's social media analysis effectively integrates promotional activities with user growth, and the data sources and analysis dimensions are comprehensive. Using Looker Studio for visual dashboards allows for real-time monitoring of the effects, and the strategy adjustments are highly targeted.

This analysis of the Thai cuisine restaurant is very accurate and realistic, precisely targeting local customers such as students and Muslims. It takes into account both the positive review keywords and the negative review pain points, and the solution is practical and highly targeted.
This is such a well-structured social media analytics plan for TamJai SamGor! I especially love how you focused on engagement rate and tied content strategy to local humor and audience behavior.

This is a really clear and practical social media analytics plan for QQ.I think your points about simplifying the interface and adding privacy features are super insightful for winning back young users.The data-driven strategy and dashboard design also make the plan easy to understand and implement.

This is a comprehensive and insightful social media analytics framework tailored perfectly for iHerb’s global wellness focus. Your emphasis on decoding user intent through the Save-to-Like ratio and prioritizing educational UGC is particularly smart, as it aligns with how health-conscious Gen Z and Millennials make purchasing decisions. The conversion funnel dashboard also effectively bridges social engagement with business outcomes, making the plan actionable for stakeholders.

This is a well-structured social media analytics plan focused on McDonald’s Instagram performance and the student demographic. Your focus on content analysis across post types and the hypothesis about Reels outperforming static photos align perfectly with the target audience’s preferences. The actionable strategies, like increasing posting cadence and collaborating with student micro-influencers, directly address the goal of boosting engagement rate.

This is such a passionate and well-thought-out social media analytics plan for Tower Records.I love how you focus on reviving physical music culture rather than just nostalgia, and the localization strategy based on each city’s unique music vibe is brilliant.Your focus on topic volume instead of simple likes truly captures the spirit of making physical music great again.

I really like your distinction between loyalty to game mechanics versus loyalty to design philosophy. This reflects a deeper understanding of brand vs product attachment. One possible extension would be to segment sentiment by user type (hardcore Wukong players vs casual followers). This could reveal whether resistance to the new theme comes from a specific sub-community rather than the audience as a whole.

Your goal of repositioning Citybus from a functional utility to an eco-friendly lifestyle brand is very clearly articulated and well matched with an engagement-based KPI. Engagement Rate is appropriate at this stage of brand transformation. One possible enhancement would be to separate passive engagement (likes) from active engagement (comments and shares), since these behaviors may reflect very different levels of brand attachment and advocacy.

Your shift from brand awareness to brand loyalty and community interaction is very well articulated, and Engagement Rate is an appropriate KPI for this objective. I especially like how you justify engagement as a proxy for resonance rather than passive exposure. You might further strengthen this by briefly noting whether different engagement actions (comments vs likes) are weighted equally, since comments often indicate deeper cognitive involvement.

Your positioning of CHAGEE as “Healthy Oriental Tea” is clearly reflected in the choice of UGC and tag usage as the core KPI. Measuring active tag adoption rather than impressions is a strong indicator of genuine brand advocacy. To strengthen this further, you might consider distinguishing between original UGC creation and participatory reposting, as these behaviors often signal different levels of community engagement.

Using comments from authoritative automotive YouTube channels (e.g., Carwow, Top Gear) is a strong choice because these audiences often shape early public opinion. Combining this with X hashtag searches provides breadth. You might enhance the methodology by noting how you will handle language differences (e.g., English vs German comments), since sentiment models can behave differently across linguistic contexts.

Engagement is a logical KPI for a platform built on personalization and interaction. However, since you include likes, comments, saves, and subscriptions together, you might consider distinguishing between low-effort engagement (likes) and high-intent engagement (saves, shares, subscriptions). These behaviors reflect different levels of commitment and may reveal more nuanced insights about user loyalty.

Your goal of increasing brand engagement is clearly defined and well aligned with Starbucks’ lifestyle-oriented positioning. I also appreciate that you define engagement rate relative to reach, which makes the metric more performance-oriented. To strengthen this section further, you might clarify whether all engagement behaviors (likes, comments, shares, saves) are treated equally, since comments and shares typically reflect deeper cognitive and emotional involvement than likes.

Your reporting section is very strong and professionally structured.One enhancement:Executives often care about financial impact, not just CTR.You could include:“Estimated incremental revenue from high-CTR content vs baseline.” Even a simple model: Additional clicks × historical conversion rate × average order value. That makes your proposal more business-driven.

Your plan for CASETiFY HK shows strong brand insight, especially in framing the brand as a cultural identity platform rather than just a product seller. The use of Google Trends to connect social engagement with search demand is a smart strategic move. To strengthen the analysis, you could clarify how the “Intent-to-Engagement Ratio” is measured (e.g., time lag and normalization) and benchmark the 0.58% engagement rate against industry standards. This would make your conclusions about the “Brand-Demand Gap” more analytically rigorous and persuasive.

Your proposal for Luckin Coffee is clear and practical, with a strong focus on engagement rate as a measurable KPI. The structure is logical and easy to follow. To improve, you could briefly explain how engagement will be standardized across Weibo, Douyin, and Xiaohongshu, since platform algorithms differ. Adding deeper analysis—such as distinguishing short-term collaboration spikes from long-term engagement growth—would also strengthen the strategic value of your recommendations.

Spot on analysis! Targeting the 18–30 age bracket with IP collaborations (like anime or gaming) is a brilliant way to drive that 'social sharing' behavior you mentioned. Your plan covers all the bases from collection to reporting. One thought: would a 'refer-a-friend' social discount fit into your strategy to help hit that 20% engagement target faster? Overall, a very professional and actionable blog post!

I really enjoyed your section on Content Analysis. Starbucks is a master of visual storytelling, so investigating which visual elements (like store aesthetics vs. product close-ups) resonate most is a smart move. If the data shows that user-generated content (UGC) drives more engagement, it could completely change their production budget. Have you considered how 'Comment Sentiment' might play into your analysis of these visuals?

Your proposal hits the nail on the head regarding the 'long version cycle' challenge. Using Engagement Rate as a proxy for player passion is a smart way to measure retention potential. One thought: since you mentioned Gen Z and Millennials, have you considered how collaborations (like the 'Divine Damsel' opera) impact the Sentiment Score? Including a metric for 'Cultural Impact' could show stakeholders the value of these high-effort artistic projects beyond just raw numbers.

Your strategy to create themed content series is excellent. SHUSHU/TONG has such a strong narrative world; using analytics to find out if 'Behind-the-scenes' stories resonate could help the brand feel more human and accessible. I’d love to see a mention of Video Completion Rate in your KPIs, especially if they increase their short-video production on Instagram Reels or XHS. This would tell you exactly where the audience loses interest in a styling video.

This is a very 'real-world' plan! Focusing on Positive Comment Rate instead of just 'Likes' shows you understand that for a legacy brand like McDonald’s, quality of engagement matters more than quantity. Your plan to optimize ad placements on Douyin based on 'Parent-Child Bonding' themes is a very actionable takeaway. This is a solid, data-driven framework that directly addresses the brand's biggest challenge in the health-conscious China market. Well done!

Your plan is great! Targeting the 18–35 age group fits well with their high activity on social media and preference for short videos and interactive topics. However, it would help to compare your performance with competitors. Consider adding a review of social media performance from brands like Costa and Luckin Coffee to strengthen your competitive strategy.

This is a very popular game! Your target audience is spot-on and matches the game's core user profile perfectly. Also, your data sources cover both the domestic and global core player communities, which helps capture interaction patterns and cultural differences across markets.  
For suggestions, you could further refine your KPIs — for example, on Bilibili, adding a "coin-to-like ratio" could help assess how engaging your content peaks are.

It covers both global  and local  platforms, which helps reach a diverse range of visitors.  Also, your report includes plenty of beautiful images — it looks great!

Perhaps discussing this limitation or proposing a method to track this like specific coupon codes mentioned in the live stream would strengthen the feasibility of your plan.

I really appreciate how you shifted the focus from vanity metrics (likes) to conversion (CTR). Perhaps using unique promo codes within the videos  or tracking the time-stamp of traffic spikes immediately after posting could help isolate the performance of each content style. I'd love to hear your thoughts on tackling this attribution challenge!

I particularly like how you connected 'drop culture' directly to your engagement goals. A suggestion regarding the Platform Strategy: You mentioned using X for awareness. Great job on this analytics plan! Here are a few constructive thoughts and suggestions to build on your excellent foundation:Platform Specificity for Hong Kong: Your audience targeting is spot on. This is a really well-structured and thoughtfully designed analytics plan!Expand the Visual Data Sources: Utilizing Xinhong and Instagram Insights is a great start for a highly visual game. Tracking trending audio or transformation transition templates could reveal a lot about how players want to showcase their outfits.Bridge Engagement and Monetization KPIs: Your current KPI perfectly measures engagement. However, since your overarching goal includes boosting gacha participation, it might be helpful to track a secondary KPI. For example, you could run sentiment analysis specifically on keywords related to monetization—like “worth it,” “pull rates,” or “expensive”—when a new clothing set drops. This helps determine if players love a design but are skipping the gacha due to perceived cost.

This is a highly structured and insightful analytics plan!Here are a few specific highlights from your plan, along with some constructive questions I'd love to discuss further:Accurate Pain Point Insight: Your concept of the "Brand-Demand Gap" is excellent. By contrasting CASETiFY against the high stickiness of Wildflower Cases, you accurately pinpointed the brand's current reality: it leans too heavily on being transactional and currently lacks community depth. Deepening Lifestyle Content: In your strategy section, you mentioned shifting 30% of content toward lifestyle storytelling to engage Gen Z and Millennials. Considering this younger demographic highly values brand authenticity and cultural identity, what specific daily pain points or high-frequency lifestyle scenarios do you think CASETiFY could leverage aside from new hardware launches to build this community depth?

This is a fantastic and highly structured analytics plan! You've clearly mapped out the user journey from social media engagement to app conversion, and the integration of external market monitoring tools like Qi Mai Data alongside native Meta insights is a very smart approach for connecting social metrics to actual app downloads.Here are some suggestiones:Audience Segmentation: Since your target demographic spans both university students and young professionals, it might be highly valuable to segment the analytics between these two groups. University students often have distinct referral behaviors compared to working professionals. Tracking which specific segment drives higher "friend invitation code" usage could help optimize future ad spend.Balancing Acquisition with Retention: You mentioned improving long-term usage in the goals, but the tracked metrics heavily focus on the first order and immediate conversion rate. It would strengthen the plan to include a metric like "Second Order Rate" or "Day-7 App Retention." This would help identify if the HKD 300 welcome coupon is bringing in loyal users or just one-time deal seekers.

here are a few constructive suggestions:You mentioned wanting to discover the “long-term sustainability” of player growth. To track this, you could add specific retention-focused KPIs, such as “30-day engagement rate post-comeback reward claiming,” to differentiate loyal returning players from those just logging in for free card packs.You highlighted Trend Analysis, which is perfect for mapping conversation spikes to promotional phases. Since you also plan to evaluate player frustration regarding game balance, explicitly adding Sentiment Analysis would make your methodology even stronger.

Your primary goal is to boost brand awareness and craft a memorable tag, but your chosen KPI is Engagement Rate. While engagement is crucial, brand awareness is typically better measured by top-of-funnel metrics like Reach, Impressions, Follower Growth, and Share of Voice. Engagement rate tells us how well the content resonates, but reach tells us how many new potential users are seeing that new tag.It's great that you are looking at platforms like Instagram and RedNote. Since building a 'memorable tag' is a core objective, you might want to explicitly mention Social Listening or Sentiment Analysis in your data collection. This helps track not just how often a tag is used, but the context and emotion behind how the audience is using it.

Your primary goal is to drive actual restaurant traffic, but your initial KPIs are online engagement. You might want to suggest a way to track the actual offline conversion. For example, giving the influencers a specific trackable promo code would help you directly tie social media engagement to the after foot traffic mentioned in your final report.While categorizing price as negative makes sense for the current problem, sentiment analysis tools usually look at the context rather than just keywords. For instance, after your campaign, someone might comment, The price is amazing with the student discount, which would actually be a positive sentiment.

You made a great choice highlighting Rednote and Instagram for unfiltered feedback. To make this step even more actionable, you could mention how you plan to gather this volume of data. For example, suggesting the use of specific social listening tools or tracking specific campaign hashtags would make the methodology stronger.You correctly pointed out that Mixue relies heavily on "emotional connections." Because of this, explicitly including Sentiment Analysis as part of your content analysis would be very powerful. By leveraging tools to categorize the text in the comments into positive, negative, or neutral emotions, you could quantify exactly how the audience feels about the brand, not just how often they interact.

One more thought on the strategy section: what about leveraging opinion leaders? Two-step Flow Model suggests that influencers and content creators act as intermediaries who shape community opinions. VALORANT's community is so diverse, so having platform-specific strategies is good!

The "blind box" model you mentioned is actually a perfect example of Reward Learning. The whole blind box concept is literally designed around dopamine hits and variable reward schedules. You don't know what you're getting, which creates that anticipation and excitement. From a neuroscience perspective, that uncertainty activates reward pathways in the brain way more than predictable outcomes. I'm curious whether your content analysis could capture this psychological mechanism? Like, do posts that emphasize mystery and surprise (showing a silhouette or teasing "something new is coming" without revealing the full figure) drive higher engagement than posts with full product reveals? That would be tapping into C0 unconscious processing—the automatic, emotional response before people even consciously think about whether they want to buy.

If your data shows that "mystery teaser" posts outperform "full reveal" posts, that would be strong evidence that POP MART's audience is driven by reward anticipation rather than just product information. That insight could shape your entire content strategy. It would be really cool to see whether the engagement data actually reflects that psychological pattern.

Haidilao × My Little Pony collab is such an unexpected combo! Really differentiate Haidilao with other hotpot restaurant.
What's particularly strong is that your strategy is actually a perfect example of the Opinion Leaders / Two-step Flow Model. When you mention "launch a creative eating method challenge with influential bloggers," you're describing the two-step flow in action. This model proposes that information doesn't flow directly from brands to mass audiences, but rather flows from brands to opinion leaders -- food bloggers, KOL, and then from those opinion leaders to their followers. Young consumers trust peer recommendations and "real" user experiences from influencers they follow way more than official corporate messaging. 
Your content analysis method could actually test this theory directly. Compare engagement rates between Haidilao's official account posts versus influencer/UGC posts within the same content categories. For example, does a food blogger's post about hidden eating methods get significantly higher engagement than Haidilao's official post about the same eating method? If so, that's direct evidence of the two-step flow model at work.

Really strong work overall! I believe what could make this even stronger is developing your own "Crazy Thursday" comparison through the lens of the Opinion Leaders / Two-step Flow Model. "Crazy Thursday" became viral because opinion leaders e.g. meme creators, food bloggers, popular accounts, transformed KFC's promotion into participatory content that spread organically through their followers, not just through KFC's official channels. Your competitor analysis may want to examine: Does KFC generate more UGC and activate more mid-tier influencers than McDonald's does? If so, that reveals a competitive gap that KFC is better at leveraging the two-step flow model. McDonald's should then shift strategy to create "meme-able" campaigns specifically designed to be remixed and shared by opinion leaders, seed content with mid-tier influencers who have engaged communities, and measure secondary content creation rather than just official account metrics. This theoretical grounding would explain why McDonald's might be underperforming and provide a clear, evidence-based strategic direction.

This social media analytics plan for Mixue is clear and well-structured, especially in how it links the brand’s youth-focused positioning to measurable performance through engagement rate. Choosing engagement rate as the main KPI makes sense for an awareness goal, since Mixue’s success depends heavily on viral content formats like mascots, memes, and short videos. The use of content analysis is also a strong methodological choice because it allows the brand to identify which creative styles (UGC, promotions, humor, product posts) generate the most interaction. However, the plan could be strengthened by adding a secondary KPI such as reach or share rate, since brand awareness is not only about interaction but also about how widely content spreads. Overall, the strategy reflects Mixue’s identity well and provides a practical path for optimizing content to build stronger awareness among young consumers.

This plan presents a clear, conversion-focused approach to social media analytics for Marriott International, with a strong emphasis on lead generation through direct bookings. Choosing conversion rate as the single KPI is especially effective because it ties social performance to real business value, rather than stopping at surface-level engagement. The plan also shows good strategic awareness by targeting high-spending travelers in Asia and North America and recognizing platform differences—especially the importance of Instagram for visuals and RED for lifestyle-driven trust and peer reviews. The use of competitor analysis is a smart framework for identifying gaps in Marriott’s content strategy, although the plan could be strengthened by clarifying how conversions will be tracked consistently across platforms and how attribution challenges (e.g., influencer impact vs. paid ads) will be handled. Overall, the study is well-structured and realistic, and its focus on actionable insights—such as KOL partnerships, optimized posting times, and stronger CTAs—makes it a practical roadmap for improving Marriott’s social-to-booking pipeline.

This social media analytics plan provides a clear and practical framework for evaluating Sushiro Hong Kong’s limited-time promotions, especially by focusing on Engagement Rate as a core KPI. The plan correctly identifies that interactive behaviors such as likes, comments, and shares can reflect consumer interest and potential dining intention, making it a suitable metric for restaurant marketing. The use of public engagement data is also realistic given the lack of backend access, and applying content analysis to compare different promotional themes is an effective way to discover what products generate the strongest audience response. However, the plan could be strengthened by considering additional contextual factors such as posting time, paid boosting, and seasonal trends, which may influence engagement performance. Overall, it is a well-structured and actionable approach that can help Sushiro optimize future campaign strategies and allocate marketing resources more efficiently.

This research plan presents a clear and practical framework for analyzing UNIQLO’s social media performance in China, especially by focusing on engagement rate as the key indicator and selecting highly relevant platforms such as Xiaohongshu and Douyin. The plan correctly identifies that UNIQLO’s strongest social media advantage lies in user-generated outfit sharing and interactive promotional mechanisms, which are highly aligned with the consumption habits of young urban audiences. Its emphasis on content observational analysis is appropriate because public metrics like likes, comments, and favorites can effectively reflect audience response and content resonance. However, the plan could be strengthened by adding more qualitative interpretation of comment sentiment and clearer benchmarking against competitors. Overall, it offers a solid strategy-oriented approach and highlights how collaborative campaigns, livestream engagement, and UGC challenges can help UNIQLO improve both visibility and consumer interaction.

This research proposal for Subway's social media analytics demonstrates a solid foundation with its clear focus on the 16-30 demographic and strategic selection of TikTok and Instagram as primary channels. This TikTok analytics plan for Luckin Coffee demonstrates a refreshingly pragmatic approach by prioritizing conversion over vanity metrics. Overall, this is a thoughtful plan that balances analytical rigor with audience understanding. With minor refinements to metric selection and competitive context, it could become even more actionable for Volkswagen Hong Kong's marketing team as they navigate an increasingly electrified and competitive automotive landscape.

Great plan overall—clear goals, practical analytics tools, and strong focus on nostalgic storytelling. Expanding metrics beyond reach and incorporating user-generated content across platforms could make the strategy even more comprehensive and impactful.

This is a well-structured and comprehensive social media analytics plan that effectively bridges campaign goals with measurable KPIs, while systematically moving from data collection to actionable insights. Two specific suggestions: consider adding cohort analysis to segment new users by acquisition source (such as specific post types) to better track long-term retention and customer lifetime value, further supporting the goal of improving long-term usage. Also, to more directly measure the viral effectiveness of the referral campaign, it would be valuable to refine sentiment analysis by explicitly tracking user-generated behaviors like sharing invitation codes (e.g., through screenshots or requests). Overall, the strategy of integrating external platform data with internal metrics demonstrates strong strategic thinking and a holistic perspective.

This is a well-focused and practical plan for understanding and improving McDonald’s brand reputation. The choice of KPIs and platforms aligns closely with the target audience’s behavior. Additionally, correlating positive comment spikes with specific local store initiatives (e.g., kitchen tours, new healthy menus) could help identify which on-ground actions most effectively drive online reputation. This would make the insight more actionable for both marketing and operations teams. Great strategic alignment overall!

This is a sharp, well-targeted plan. Focusing on brand sentiment as the core KPI perfectly aligns with NetEase Cloud Music’s community-centric value. This would pinpoint what specifically drives loyalty or frustration, making your strategic recommendations even more actionable. Great work!

Excellent framework for moving from gut feeling to data-driven action. Your focus on engagement rate over vanity metrics is spot-on for measuring authentic brand connection. One potential enhancement: consider tracking engagement not just by category, but by time of day and day of week for your target urban professionals. This could reveal the optimal schedule to pair your high-performing format with the sustainability message, maximizing its impact. Great work linking analysis directly to a creative prescriptive action.

This is a strong, focused foundation. To build on it, consider moving beyond reach as your primary KPI. For a brand like Starbucks, measuring Engagement Rate (likes, comments, shares / reach) alongside reach would reveal not just if content is seen, but if it truly resonates—a key distinction for a lifestyle brand. This would help differentiate between broad visibility and meaningful connection.

I really like how you’ve centered the entire plan around emotional resonance rather than just chasing likes or views. Using Net Sentiment Score as the KPI makes perfect sense for a campaign like “Mai Mai Farm,” where the goal is literally to change how people feel. The way you plan to use LLMs to analyze comments and danmu at scale is also super practical—manually coding that much emotional data would be a nightmare. One thing I’d be curious about: since Douyin’s algorithm rewards watch time and replays, do you think there’s a risk that the most “healing” videos might actually get deprioritized if they’re too slow-paced? Maybe cross-referencing sentiment with retention curves could help fine-tune the storytelling without losing the sincerity. Really solid work overall.

You’ve really nailed the balance between hype and logistics here—which is exactly what POP MART needs, given how much of their business lives in that tension between scarcity marketing and clear purchase rules. I love that you’re treating Facebook and Instagram as distinct tools rather than just cross-posting the same thing; that shows real strategic thinking. One thing to consider adding: since “drop culture” thrives on FOMO, it would be valuable to analyze the time gap between announcement posts and actual sell-out moments. Even without exact inventory data, tracking when comments shift from “when/where” to “sold out already” could reveal the optimal lead time for building hype without frustrating your audience. That kind of timing insight would make your cadence recommendations even sharper. Really solid plan.

You’ve built a really solid, insight-driven plan here—the focus on engagement rate over vanity metrics makes total sense for a brand like Nike that thrives on community and emotional connection. I especially appreciate how clearly you’ve connected each section, from goal setting all the way to reporting; it flows naturally and feels actionable. One layer you might consider adding is a deeper dive into audience segmentation within the 18–35 range. A college athlete and a working professional in their early 30s might both follow Nike, but they probably engage with different types of content. If your data allows, breaking out engagement patterns by age bracket or even by platform behavior could reveal more precise content angles—like whether TikTok skews toward sneaker culture while Instagram favors motivational storytelling. That kind of nuance would make your analysis even sharper.

Really solid structure—especially the way you segmented content strategy by age group within the 18–34 range. That shows real audience nuance. Tracking whether positive sentiment actually aligns with engagement spikes would give you a fuller picture—not just what people click, but how they actually feel. Also appreciate that you built in ongoing monitoring; too many plans treat analytics as a one-off report. Nice work.

Reply:Excellent point. We'll add a "cross-platform synergy" dimension, tracking if viral Xiaohongshu posts drive related queries in Douyin livestreams, and vice versa. This shows how interest moves between platforms. Thanks for the sharp suggestion!

Your inclusion of competitor analysis is a strategic strength in this plan. The data strategy in this Mixue plan is particularly strong and practical. Proposing to visualize the performance gap between IP‑driven and product‑only content with comparative bar charts would make the findings instantly actionable. This blog offers great insights into Game Science’s transition toward building a sustainable franchise. I really like this analytics plan for Spotify! This deep-dive analysis of Heytea’s strategy is really insightful, especially the key point that mere numbers cannot explain why a trend happens—we must analyze the content carrier. I think this social media analytics plan for Diptyque is really well-designed, especially its perfect choice of Trend Analysis as the main method. A clear, concise, and realistic proposal. The methodology for comparing different content formats against specific collaboration campaigns is straightforward and directly ties back to the main goal of increasing brand awareness. Great job keeping the strategy actionable!

This is a highly practical and well-localized social media strategy. This is a very thorough and systematic 5-step plan. Highlighting the specific platforms used in China (Weibo, Xiaohongshu, WeChat) makes it highly relevant to the local market. I particularly appreciate the emphasis on data cleaning—removing bot interactions and paid ads is a crucial step that many overlook, but it is essential for getting accurate engagement insights.

This is a highly comprehensive and practical plan for Keeta. I really appreciate how you linked social media metrics from Meta with actual app download trends using Qi Mai Data. Tying top-of-funnel social engagement directly to bottom-line conversion rates and first-order volume is a fantastic, data-driven approach.

Strengths:Highly Relevant Context: It accurately captures the unique situation of Hearthstone’s server shutdown and its subsequent return to the Chinese market. The goals—re-engaging lapsed players and rebuilding brand loyalty—are highly practical and appropriate.

Localized and Professional Data Sources: It precisely identifies key Chinese social media platforms (Bilibili, Douyin, etc.) and the official community hub (NetEase Dashen), while also incorporating professional card deck data sources (HSReplay, HSGuru). This ensures rich and relevant data dimensions.

Clear Action-Orientation: The “Strategy & Action” section effectively links potential data insights (e.g., frustration with game balance) to concrete marketing or PR actions (e.g., transparent developer communication), demonstrating strong practical value.

Areas for Improvement:

Specify Tools and Methodologies: In the “Data Collection” and “Analysis” sections, the plan would appear much more professional if it specified the actual Social Listening Tools or sentiment analysis algorithms it intends to use to process large volumes of text.

Audience Segmentation: Defining the audience simply as “male gamers aged 18-35” is a bit broad. Given that this is a relaunch campaign, the plan would be stronger if it segmented the audience into categories like “hardcore returning players,” “casual lapsed players,” and “completely new players,” setting specific KPIs for each group.

Summary: This is a standard, well-structured business and marketing analytics blog post. It accurately identifies the core pain points and proposes highly practical solutions. By adding just a few more technical execution details, it would serve as an excellent, ready-to-use strategy document.

Strengths:Strong Competitive Benchmarking: Framing the entire study around a competitor analysis (comparing CHICHA with HeyTea and Comebuy) is a highly effective approach for a saturated market like Hong Kong. It provides a clear baseline for what "good" looks like in that specific vertical.

Platform and Audience Alignment: Identifying Instagram as the sole focus is a smart, targeted choice. For premium milk tea brands relying on aesthetics, lifestyle marketing, and visual appeal for the 18–35 demographic in Hong Kong, Instagram is undoubtedly the most critical battleground.

Areas for Improvement:



Flawed Data Collection Methodology: The plan states it will collect competitor data (like audience demographics) using "Instagram Insights." This is technically impossible, as native Insights are only available to account administrators. The plan must specify which third-party social listening or analytics tools (e.g., Emplifi, HypeAuditor, or Phlanx) it will use to estimate competitor metrics.

Vague Strategy Section: The "Strategy and Reporting" section is underdeveloped. While previous sections are specific, the strategy merely states the brand can "adjust its social media strategy." It needs to offer hypothetical, actionable recommendations (e.g., "If HeyTea's daily posting yields lower engagement than CHICHA's tri-weekly posting, recommend maintaining a lower frequency but higher production value").

Slight Metric Misalignment: The stated goal is to boost "brand awareness," which is typically measured by Reach, Impressions, or Follower Growth. However, the chosen KPI is "engagement rate." The plan should either shift the primary goal to "brand affinity/loyalty" or include reach-based metrics in the KPI list.

Strengths:Deep Platform Understanding: The plan demonstrates a superb grasp of Bilibili’s unique cultural ecosystem. The inclusion of platform-specific metrics and features like "UP主" (creators), "弹幕" (bullet comments), "一键三连" (like, coin, favorite), and community "黑话" (insider language) makes the strategy highly customized and authentic.

Strategic Focus on Mid-Tier Creators: Targeting mid-tier creator retention is a very smart and realistic business objective. Instead of just focusing on top influencers, nurturing the "middle class" of creators is exactly what a UGC-driven platform needs to sustain its content ecosystem.

Innovative Reporting Methods: Proposing "Content Heatmaps" to visualize where bullet comments cluster within a video is a brilliant, platform-tailored idea that would provide highly actionable insights for video pacing and editing.

Areas for Improvement:

Missing Competitive Tracking: While the introduction rightly identifies Douyin and Kuaishou as major threats, the "Data Collection" and "Analysis" sections are purely inward-looking. The plan should include a metric for cross-platform competitive analysis (e.g., tracking if mid-tier creators are migrating to or dual-posting on rival platforms).

Vague Qualitative Analysis Execution: The plan mentions scraping bullet comments and user comments but doesn't specify how this massive amount of unstructured data will be analyzed. Naming specific Natural Language Processing (NLP) tools or sentiment analysis frameworks capable of understanding Gen-Z slang would make this section much stronger.

Strengths:Highly Quantifiable Goals: Setting a specific, time-bound baseline and target for the KPI (increasing the engagement rate from 2.8% to 4.5% within 3 months) makes the plan highly measurable and grounded in business reality.

Outstanding Reporting Framework: The multi-tiered reporting structure is exceptional. Providing weekly email briefs, monthly reports, quarterly management presentations, and an interactive dashboard ensures that stakeholders at all levels get the right data at the right time.

Clear Strategic Alignment: The proposed actions perfectly align with the stated goal. To penetrate second and third-tier cities, the recommendation to use localized content and collaborate with local KOCs (Key Opinion Consumers) is a highly effective and practical tactic.

Areas for Improvement:

Missing a Critical Platform: For a visually-driven food and lifestyle brand like HEYTEA, omitting Xiaohongshu (Little Red Book) from the "Primary Data Sources" is a noticeable gap. Xiaohongshu is arguably the most important platform for driving beverage trends and UGC among young Chinese consumers.

Geographical Data Filtering: While the target audience is strictly defined as users in second and third-tier cities, the "Data Collection" and "Analysis" sections do not explain how the data will be geographically segmented. It is crucial to outline how the team will filter out the noise from top-tier cities (like Beijing or Shanghai) to ensure the insights actually reflect the target market.

Strengths:Strong Platform-Audience Fit: Choosing Xiaohongshu as the primary channel is highly strategic. The platform’s heavy focus on lifestyle, fitness, and diet “seeding” (种草) perfectly aligns with the target demographic of health-conscious, time-poor professionals in Tier-1 cities.Detailed Persona Development: The plan does an excellent job of segmenting the audience into two distinct but overlapping groups (hardcore fitness enthusiasts vs. busy professionals facing sub-health risks) who share a common pain point: the need for quick, convenient protein.

Clear, Data-Backed Actions: The strategy directly answers the findings of the analysis. Moving from static images to video formats and pivoting toward brand collaborations based on historical engagement data shows a logical, evidence-based approach to content planning.Areas for Improvement:



Goal vs. KPI Misalignment: The stated primary objective is to "drive traffic" (which usually implies reach, impressions, or click-throughs to a storefront), but the chosen KPI is "engagement rate" (likes, comments, shares). The plan should either redefine the goal as "boosting brand interaction" or add specific traffic/conversion metrics to the KPI list.

Underestimating Xiaohongshu's KOC Ecosystem: The analysis concludes that brand collaborations outperform influencer content and thus focuses heavily on brand co-marketing. However, Xiaohongshu is fundamentally driven by Key Opinion Consumers (KOCs) and authentic peer reviews. Completely side-lining influencer collaborations might be a missed opportunity; it would be better to analyze why past influencer content failed and optimize influencer selection instead.

Methodological Inconsistency: In the "Data Collection" section, the plan explicitly states it adopts a "quantitative analysis approach." Yet, in the "Reporting" section, it mentions "extracting keywords," which is a qualitative method (text/sentiment analysis). The methodology needs to be unified.

This is a very professional and well-organized social media analysis plan for Hong Kong Disneyland. You have a very clear vision for the brand. Here are my comments:
Strengths
Your plan is very easy to read. I really like how you divided your Target Audience into two groups: young people and parents. This is very smart because these two groups care about different things. Also, your choice of Sentiment Analysis is perfect for a famous brand like Disneyland. It is very important to know how people feel about their visit.
Areas for Improvement
The plan is very strong, but perhaps you could look at the Data Source section one more time. You mentioned Twitter (now called X), but in Hong Kong, many young people and tourists use Xiaohongshu or YouTube to share their park experiences. Including these might give you even more useful data.
Suggestions for Revision
To make this even better, I sincerely suggest adding one or two more platforms that are popular in the local market. For example, Xiaohongshu is great for seeing "beautiful photos" from young visitors. Also, in your Reporting section, you could suggest using a Word Cloud chart. This is a very simple and clear way to show which words (like "long queues" or "friendly dolls") appear most often in reviews.
You have done a great job on this assignment!
Follow-up: Would you like to look at which social media platforms are most popular for tourists visiting Hong Kong right now?

Strengths
First, your Core Objectives and KPIs are very clear. They provide a strong direction for the brand. Second, your Target Audience analysis is excellent. You clearly understand that young people use TikTok and Instagram frequently. Finally, the Data Visualization section is very smart. Using heat maps and word clouds will make the data easy for managers to understand.
Areas for Improvement
The report is very strong, but perhaps you could look at the Localization Trend Analysis section again. It mentions store numbers in Singapore and the US, but it might be helpful to include even more specific details about local coffee tastes in those areas.
Suggestions for Revision
To make this even better, I sincerely suggest adding a small section on Cultural Risks. Every country has different traditions, so it is important to check if your advertisements fit the local culture perfectly. You could also include a few specific examples of "local expressions" for the Singapore or US markets to show exactly how the tone will change.
Overall, this is a high-quality analysis. Great job!
Follow-up: Would you like me to help you brainstorm some specific localized slogans for the Singapore or US markets?

Overall, it’s a solid, well-organized plan that already works as a good template, and with a bit more emphasis on the business funnel and actionable experimentation, it could be genuinely execution-ready.

Labeling posts into categories like value deals, new items, customization tips, behind-the-scenes prep, creator taste tests, and customer content is a very practical approach, and comparing engagement across these buckets is exactly what brands should be doing but often don’t.

As a reader, I think this Zara piece is very clear, sharp, and commercial-focused. Overall, it reads like a concise, ROI-driven plan that a real management team could actually use.

After reading this, I think the you pinpoints Game Science’s real challenge: not just creating another hit, but turning Wukong’s hype into a lasting “Black Myth universe” and a strong studio brand. Using cross‑pollination rate to see whether existing Wukong fans actually engage with Zhongkui content is a smart, focused KPI, and the use of social listening and retention data helps clarify whether players are loyal to a character or to the studio. My only suggestion is to make the “universe building” strategy more concrete, with clearer content types, ratios, and timelines to make it easier to execute.

I feel your plan for Spotify is clear, focused, and well aligned with how the brand already behaves online. You anchor everything around engagement as the main KPI, which fits perfectly with Spotify’s strength in shareable, personalized content like Wrapped and custom playlists. I like that you’ve clearly defined the 18–30 audience not just by age but by behaviors—expressing emotions, chasing trends, and responding to visually appealing, personal content—because that directly informs the kind of creative you propose. Your content analysis idea (comparing Wrapped, voting playlists, artist endorsements, and UGC) is very sensible, and your hypothesis that personalization and storytelling will drive the strongest response is consistent with how people already use Spotify socially. My main suggestion would be to tie engagement a bit more explicitly to downstream actions, for example tracking how specific high‑engagement post types correlate with clicks to playlists, follows of artists, or starts of free trials/premium upgrades, so stakeholders see not just “more buzz,” but clearer business impact.

I think your Haidilao plan captures the brand’s social nature quite well: you clearly aim to “drive traffic and generate leads,” and your chosen KPIs—new follower growth and engagement conversion rate—match that goal better than pure awareness metrics would. I like that you define the 18–30 audience not only by age but by lifestyle (early career, frequent social gatherings, valuing both taste and atmosphere), because it supports your focus on topics like late‑night dining and single-person sets. The trend analysis idea—tracking food-related hashtags and then riding waves like “DIY Soup Base” or “Late-night Dining”—fits Haidilao’s flexible, experiential image, and your examples (influencers making custom soup bases, hashtag check-ins for free drinks/food) are concrete and easy to execute. My main suggestion would be to tighten the link between your social trends and in-store or online conversions: for example, using unique promo codes or trackable links tied to each trend campaign, so your “engagement conversion rate” is clearly defined and stakeholders can see which topics actually move people from liking a post to visiting a restaurant or placing an order.

After reading this blog, I feel your Pizza Hut plan is structured, practical, and well aligned with how QSR brands actually compete online. Your goal–KPI setup makes sense: focusing on engagement rate as the core metric, with sentiment score as a secondary check on brand perception, fits an 18–30 audience that cares about fun, shareable content and deals. I like that you define this group by behavior—convenience, novelty, social sharing—and tie that directly to platforms like TikTok and Rednote, where interactive formats and promotions work best. Your decision to center the analysis on competitor benchmarking against Domino’s and Papa John’s is smart: it gives you a concrete way to spot content gaps and “borrow” proven campaign themes, such as delivery-focused interactive ideas. The strategies you propose—UGC challenges like “Create Your Pizza Hut Day,” IP collaborations with anime/films/games, and stronger community management—are all very on-brand and realistic to execute, especially if you pair each campaign with its own hashtag and trackable links so you can attribute engagement lifts and traffic spikes accurately. My suggestion would be to sharpen the connection between your 20% engagement target and specific levers, so stakeholders can see which initiatives are likely to drive the biggest share of that improvement and prioritize resources accordingly.

After reading this, I find your Starbucks Hong Kong plan concise and well focused on what matters: boosting Facebook engagement through a clear, single KPI. Using content analysis to compare Reels, product close‑ups, and UGC is very practical, and your strategies—doubling down on high‑performing formats, rethinking weak seasonal campaigns around functional benefits, and adjusting posting times—are straightforward to execute. My only suggestion is to add one lightweight “business” metric, such as clicks to store info or promotions, so engagement ties a bit more directly to real-world visits.

Your plan does a nice job defining a clear audience, choosing fitting platforms (Bilibili, Twitter/X, YouTube), and using content categories like “lore-heavy” to guide strategy. The author provided a detailed introduction to the operational logic of Bilibili, and as an uploader, I would like to add some additional information. Bilibili is currently making adjustments by reducing the regular creative incentives, but increasing the monthly fan acquisition incentive, which can improve the quality of UGC content.
