I also found the focus on identifying content patterns that trigger the community’s “一键三连” behavior very insightful and very “Bilibili-like,” which shows you understand the platform culture well. Overall, this is a clear and practical plan, and the recommendations for creator incentives and algorithm improvements feel actionable and relevant to Bilibili’s current competitive challenges.

I went to HongKong Disneyland last National day.This is a clear and practical analytics plan for Hong Kong Disneyland. I like how you focused the goal on increasing positive social media reviews, because visitor sentiment and online word-of-mouth are very important for theme parks. Your identification of two key audience groups—young visitors who enjoy sharing photos and experiences, and parents who care about safety and children’s enjoyment—feels very realistic and fits well with Disneyland’s typical visitors. Using sentiment analysis is also a sensible choice since people’s emotional reactions to their visit strongly influence whether others want to go. I also appreciate the simple strategy of promoting strengths while improving commonly mentioned problems, which makes the plan easy to implement. Overall, this is a focused and easy-to-follow plan that connects social media insights with practical actions for improving visitor perception.

This is a very bold and engaging analytics plan for DJI. I really like how you framed the goal around lead generation and real purchase intent rather than simple awareness metrics, which makes the strategy feel much more business-driven. The idea of tracking search index growth after video exposure is particularly smart because it captures genuine user interest instead of passive views. I also think your insight about fear being the main barrier for new drone users is interesting and realistic. Using Douyin’s backend tools like Ocean Engine Trend Insight and audience retention data provides a strong analytical foundation, and your strategy of shifting the message toward beginner-friendly features and relatable creators feels very practical. Overall, this is a creative and confident plan that connects data insights with a clear marketing direction for expanding DJI’s audience.

QQ is a memorable app for me.This is a clear and thoughtful analytics plan for QQ. I like how you focused on the decline of monthly active users and the competitive pressure from platforms like WeChat and Douyin, which makes the analysis very relevant to QQ’s current situation. Your use of trend analysis on MAU over several years is a sensible approach because it clearly shows how user behavior has been changing over time. I also appreciate that you highlighted young users under 35 as the core audience, since this group has historically been QQ’s strongest community. The suggested actions, such as simplifying the interface and improving privacy features in QQ Space, feel practical and directly connected to user needs. Overall, this is a well-structured plan that uses data trends to support realistic strategies for improving QQ’s user engagement.

Love the smart call to split "good pain" from "bad pain" for Soulslike sentiment analysis—total game-changer for understanding this community. The community validation strategy is perfect for the hardcore fanbase, and tying insights to actionable dev updates makes this super practical. By the way, I think add audience segment-specific sentiment & topic analysis in the future research may be a good choice.

This plan excels at translating brand core values into data-driven action and it's a masterclass for IP-driven lifestyle brands. And I think add IP affinity segmentation to content analysis may be a good idea.

Haha, I just went to Disneyland last month. This is a well-structured social media analytics plan for Hong Kong Disneyland, with great touches like dual-platform data collection. A suggestion to boost strategic precision is to expand your ER formula to include saves and shares and add audience-segmented content performance analysis in your Content Analysis phase.

This is such a sharp blog for Café de Coral. Focusing on saves/shares over just likes is a brilliant call for measuring actual content resonance with budget-conscious, aesthetic-driven Hong Kong Gen Z, and the Type A/B content split is super clear for identifying what’s dragging the brand’s social vibe down. And I think adding competitive benchmark analysis to your Comparative Content Analysis can help your feed optimization recommendations even more data-driven and persuasive for stakeholders.

I think the Intent-to-Engagement Ratio, which connects social interactions to search demand, is a metric that fits perfectly with the brand's tech-fashion positioning. Looking forward to more detailed analysis.

This social media analytics plan for Haidilao is highly targeted and practical, with clear data sources and actionable strategies tailored to young consumers on Xiaohongshu and Douyin. It would be even more insightful if add specific metrics for tracking engagement growth and in-store conversion after implementing these IP co-branding and content strategies.

This social media analytics framework for HEYTEA is clear and actionable, with a strong focus on engagement rate and cross-platform user behavior. It could be further strengthened by adding sentiment analysis and conversion tracking to better link social insights with actual sales performance.

I often eat McDonald's in Hong Kong. And I think the segmentation by content type and golden posting time is quite spot-on, it’s very practical for McDonald’s high-frequency output! Adding competitor comparison could further help identify unique differentiation points.
I love how this UGC and IP-focused plan perfectly captures Mixue’s fun, viral brand energy! It would be even more amazing if you included real social trend forecasting.

I think this data-driven social media plan for Apple is super professional and well-structured, especially the competitor benchmarking part! Combining user experience analysis with privacy-focused content would make the strategy even more powerful.

I absolutely love how this Keeta analytics plan links coupon campaigns to multi-source data tracking and conversion-focused KPIs—it’s so targeted for the Hong Kong takeout market! Adding competitor benchmarking for takeout discount strategies will further boost the campaign’s differentiation and user conversion efficiency.

I’m obsessed with how this ZA Bank plan nails the core of virtual banking marketing—targeting Hong Kong’s digital natives and leaning into competitor benchmarking! Adding interactive financial literacy snippets would make it even more resonant for Gen Z and Millennials.

Great focus on trend analysis, a smart move in the vibrant and competitive global coffee market! Using reach as the KPI makes perfect sense for expanding Starbucks’ online content visibility. The plan is practical, linking trend insights directly to content optimization and posting schedule adjustments. It’s a clear, actionable roadmap for Starbucks’ social media growth.

Great focus on competitor analysis, a brilliant strategy in the fiercely competitive global luxury hospitality market! Using conversion rate as the core KPI is perfectly aligned with driving direct bookings and tangible business results. The plan is highly practical, linking competitive insights directly to KOL collaborations and content optimization. It’s a clear, actionable roadmap for Marriott’s social media lead generation and global growth.

Perfectly tailored to Diptyque’s artistic, niche luxury vibe, this plan shines! Trend analysis is a smart fit for its seasonal releases and aesthetic storytelling, while focusing on engagement rate as KPI perfectly measures content resonance. Practical and actionable, it’s a clear roadmap to amplify the brand’s charm and stand out in the luxury fragrance space.

This proposal on the social media analysis of Starbucks in Hong Kong is quite well-written and represents a very practical and valuable plan. You clearly has a clear understanding of the brand and the market. The target audience is precisely positioned as Hong Kong professionals aged 25 to 45, and the platform's choice of Facebook is very precise. Focusing on the core indicator of "interaction rate" is also very pragmatic, as it can directly measure the effectiveness of the content. The analytical framework is well-designed. By using "content analysis" to deconstruct Starbucks' visually-driven posts (such as drink shots, seasonal marketing, store ambiance), and attempting to identify the content types and visual elements that evoke the strongest resonance, this approach is correct. The final strategic recommendations are also based on the possible analysis results, providing actionable directions such as increasing Reels production, optimizing promotional themes, and adjusting release times. The overall logic is clear, covering from goal setting, data collection, analysis to application, forming a complete closed loop.

This TikTok operation plan is extremely excellent, demonstrating clear strategic thinking and the ability to implement effectively. The data analysis part is very detailed. Even comments like "How to book?" and "Is it suitable for couples?" are regarded as important signals, and they can even be linked to the mini-program clicks. This enables the analysis to truly serve the business growth. The content classification is also completely based on the user perspective: how to play, where to go, how much it costs, and how to book. It is step by step and extremely practical. The proposed action suggestions are specific and feasible. For instance, establishing standardized templates for efficient content, optimizing booking guidance phrases, and setting up weekly data review and rapid iteration mechanisms can all directly guide daily operations and enhance efficiency.

This blog directly connects the hot topics on Instagram with the actual search demands on Google. This leads to a key discovery: Brands are very good at creating hot topics, which means attracting people to "watch"， but after people finish watching， the sense of brand involvement and following that makes them want to delve deeper may still not be sufficient. In simple terms， it's about how to become more "popular" after "getting positive reviews". 
The suggestions provided were also very appropriate: For instance, when promoting the iPhone 17 phone case next time, instead of just advertising, you could talk about the future of technological fashion and make the brand a "must-have". Also, don't apply the same effort to each associated IP. Some can be used to retain existing customers, while others need to be heavily focused on content creation and storytelling. The last point is particularly well-received - talk more about lifestyle and stories to make the brand more "personable" rather than just a "product".

This blog zeroes in on the core of game companies - players' emotions. Instead of stubbornly focusing on those superficial exposure data, it concentrates on analyzing whether people are "ecstatic" or "heartbroken". This approach is particularly apt because Nintendo sells happiness and memories. Moreover, the entire plan is very "down-to-earth". From where to collect data (X and YouTube), to how to categorize and analyze it, and then to how to specifically respond to fans and adjust the posting rhythm, every step is crystal clear. The team can start working directly upon picking it up, and it is highly operational.

As a regular reader, this blog perfectly captures my feelings: The products of Chagee are undoubtedly excellent, but the content posted by the Instagram account in Hong Kong is simply uninteresting. It's all about stiff product images, far from the kind of content on Xiaohongshu that tells stories about the origin and discusses local flavors. This analysis hits the nail on the head: Boss Tea Goddess doesn't lack popularity, but what it lacks is a "local story" that can resonate with Hong Kong people. If it follows this approach for improvement, its social media will definitely shift from the current "ordinary" status to becoming a powerful engine that attracts people to visit the store.

The data collection approach of this blog is quite comprehensive. It includes backend data such as Instagram and Xiaohongshu, uses third-party tools to analyze trends and competitors, and also considers conducting questionnaires to directly ask fans what IP they like and what kind of content they want. The "listening to users' direct opinions" part is a strong point.

I think the design of KPIs in this blog is particularly outstanding. "Interaction retention rate" not only considers whether users come in, but also whether they continue to post comments and remarks in the latter part of the video. This indicator precisely measures whether the content has really "retained" the audience. For the popular deep videos and knowledge-based content on Bilibili at present, this is more meaningful than the completion rate.

There is one aspect that I really appreciate about it. It has accurately identified the two most crucial issues that Luckin is currently facing: which collaborations are truly effective and which content formats are genuinely popular. Luckin has conducted numerous collaborations over the past two years, and it is worthwhile to take the time to analyze which ones have truly stimulated interaction. This in itself is a very valuable thing.

I think this blog has an extremely detailed understanding of the target audience. Instead of simply saying "young people aged 18-30", it divides the audience into two categories: one is those who are truly engaged in fitness and need to quickly replenish protein, and the other is office workers who want to stay healthy but have no time to cook and are afraid of gaining weight. This distinction is extremely important because although both of these groups buy beef jerky, their underlying motivations are completely different, and the subsequent content strategies also need to be considered separately. Being able to make this distinction shows that the author has truly understood the users' living conditions.

I fully agree with the selection of trend analysis as the core method in this blog, because sustainable fashion is indeed evolving rapidly. Last month we were discussing eco-friendly fabrics, and this month it might have shifted to carbon footprint. Using trend analysis to identify hot topics enables the brand to always be at the center of the conversation. The suggestions based on the analysis are also very practical. It's not just saying "make more videos", but specific suggestions like "shoot Reels showing the process of product transformation" and "post images and text explaining the recycling of fabrics". Even the platform to use, the specific function, and the time period for posting have all been planned out.

Choosing Spotify Wrapped as the starting point is a very smart move. The fact that Wrapped becomes a trending topic on social media every December is indeed a phenomenon. People share their playlists, compare their tastes, and even make jokes about their favorite songs of the year. This kind of spontaneous dissemination ability is something that many brands can only envy. The author focuses the analysis on "how to make this sharing happen more often".

This is a practical and realistic plan that starts from real experiences. Every step is centered around the core issue of "how to make more people notice the limited products". The analytical approach is very clear. The past 60 days' posts are categorized by content, including product features, kitchen anecdotes, user reposts, etc. See which one gets more interaction from everyone. This "categorical comparison" method, although simple, is often the most effective.

This blog has a good understanding of the content type of game walkthroughs. Unlike short videos or influencer posts, the essence of a walkthrough is a "tool" that users come to use to solve problems, not to waste time. Therefore, the focus of analysis lies in dimensions such as "information density", "visual aids", and "clearness of the title", rather than just judging whether the cover is attractive or the copy is emotionally appealing. It was then discovered that walkthroughs with images perform better than pure text because players don't have the patience to read lengthy explanations when playing games. Specific problem walkthroughs (such as how to start as a new player or how to defeat a certain boss) are easier to be saved than general content; and writing the title clearly with the game name and version is more effective than being mysterious - these conclusions can directly be used to guide creators.

This blog is entirely based on real feedback: students think it's expensive, want to try it but are afraid to go. The author directly listened to what the students were complaining about in the comments sections of Xiaohongshu and Instagram. The result was clear: they like the food and the service, but they give up when it comes to the price. This contradiction of "loving it but can't afford it" is precisely where marketing can strike. The suggestions in the plan are particularly interesting: "Student Budget Challenge", find bloggers to show receipts and food, to prove that with 200 Hong Kong dollars, one can also have a satisfying meal. This creative idea uses facts to dispel concerns and is more persuasive than simply offering discounts.

The author mentioned in the plan that the visual elements of the "Twelve Beauties" are the core assets. I think this judgment is very accurate. The static images, the moments that blend classical and modern styles, are precisely what make audiences take out their phones to take pictures and post on Little Red Book. Then it pointed out that the main audience of the dance drama are Gen Z in first- and second-tier cities, who are interested in "new Chinese style" aesthetics. Those who are active on Little Red Book indicate that this group is not passively watching the drama, but actively participating in the dissemination and creation of content. The analytical method used a dual framework of content analysis and sentiment analysis. Content analysis decodes which visual elements are shared the most, while sentiment analysis delves deep into what exactly the audience is moved by and discussing. The two dimensions complement each other, enabling a clear understanding of "what is popular" and "why it is popular".

This is a solid breakdown of how Starbucks approaches social media. I think focusing on engagement rate as the main KPI makes a lot of sense here — for a brand like Starbucks, it's less about hard conversions and more about keeping people emotionally connected to the brand. One thing I'd be curious about is how you'd handle the difference between "passive" engagement (likes) and "active" engagement (comments, shares). A post might rack up thousands of likes but barely get any comments, and I feel like those two scenarios tell very different stories about how the audience is actually responding. Maybe weighting them differently in the analysis could give a clearer picture. Also, the point about filtering out spam and automated activity is important but probably trickier than it sounds in practice — would love to hear more about what criteria you'd use to draw that line. Overall though, the plan flows well from data collection to action, which is exactly what a good analytics framework should do.

Cool framing — treating this as a brand loyalty problem rather than just a game marketing plan makes it way more interesting. The Cross-Pollination Rate KPI is clever, but I'd be careful about distinguishing between fans who carry over from Wukong vs. people who just find Zhongkui's folklore cool on its own. Those look the same in engagement numbers but mean very different things for franchise health.Also worth noting — the "Universe Building" approach cuts both ways. If Zhongkui disappoints, tying the two IPs together too closely could drag Wukong's reputation down with it. Sometimes a little distance between titles is safer.Solid plan though, curious to see where this goes.

Spotify Wrapped is probably the single best example of a product feature that doubles as a social media campaign, so there's a lot to dig into here. One thing I'd push back on a bit is using "engagement" as the KPI without breaking it down further. Likes and saves mean pretty different things — a like is passive, but a save or share suggests someone actually wants to revisit or spread the content, which matters way more for a platform that lives on discovery. It might also be worth looking at how Wrapped engagement compares to Spotify's regular content throughout the year, because if the brand basically goes quiet for 11 months and then spikes every December, that's a strategy problem worth addressing. Otherwise a clean and readable plan, just could use a bit more specificity in the analysis section to stand out.

Tying specific trending topics like "DIY Soup Base" or "Late-night Dining" directly to actionable menu and promotion decisions makes the strategy feel grounded rather than abstract. One thing I'd think about though is whether follower growth rate is really the best KPI for a restaurant chain. Someone can follow Haidilao on Instagram and never actually walk into a store, so it might be worth tracking something closer to actual conversion, like coupon redemptions from social posts or check-in hashtag usage rates. Also, the influencer strategy makes sense but Haidilao already has a ton of organic UGC from customers filming the service and table-side performances — leaning into that existing content might be more cost-effective and authentic than paid partnerships. Overall a fun read though, the trend-responsive approach fits Haidilao's brand really well.

The plan reads well but one thing that stands out is the assumption that Starbucks' audience is fairly uniform — in reality, a 19-year-old college student posting an aesthetic latte pic on Instagram and a 34-year-old professional grabbing coffee before work are engaging with the brand for completely different reasons. Treating them as one segment might average out insights in a way that hides what's actually going on. It could be worth splitting the analysis by sub-groups to see if certain content types only perform well with one slice of the audience. Another thought — the reporting section mentions charts and tables but doesn't say much about how often this analysis would repeat. A one-time snapshot is useful, but Starbucks runs so many seasonal campaigns that a monthly or quarterly cadence would catch shifts that a single report would miss entirely.

Really solid breakdown of the strategy here. The IP collab idea with anime and gaming franchises is super smart, especially for the 18-30 crowd on TikTok and rednote. One thing I'd add is maybe tracking how UGC from those "Create Your Pizza Hut Day" challenges actually converts to sales, not just engagement. Sometimes viral content doesn't always translate to orders. But overall, love the structured approach and the competitor analysis angle with Domino's. Good stuff!

Nice proposal! Starbucks HK is a great pick for this kind of study since their Facebook content is already so visual and polished. I think the content analysis angle makes a lot of sense here, especially comparing Reels vs static posts. One thing that might be worth looking into is how their competitors like Pacific Coffee or local indie cafés are doing on Facebook too, just to give some context to the numbers. Also curious whether the seasonal items like mooncake collections or holiday drinks actually drive more comments than regular menu posts. Looking forward to seeing your findings!

This is such a cool topic to pick for a social media analytics study. The point about tracking Danmu density on Bilibili is really interesting since that's something most Western-focused analyses completely overlook, but it's honestly where a huge chunk of the core fanbase lives. I also like how you categorized content into lore, combat, and community types. From what I've seen in the Genshin community, lore content almost always gets the most heated discussions, especially around Archon quests. One suggestion though, it might be worth including Reddit or HoYoLAB as additional data sources since a lot of the deeper fan discussions happen there rather than on Twitter. Really enjoyed reading this, good luck with the analysis!

Great choice of brand for this study! SHUSHU/TONG has such a distinct aesthetic that it practically begs for content analysis. I think the comparison between polished campaign shots and more casual OOTD styling content will be really telling, because from what I've noticed on Xiaohongshu, users tend to engage way more with real outfit posts than editorial-style photos. Also love that you mentioned working with KOCs instead of just big influencers, that feels much more aligned with the brand's vibe. One thing you might want to consider is tracking how engagement shifts around fashion week periods vs normal times, since designer brands usually see a big spike in attention during those windows. Really well-structured plan overall, excited to see what patterns come out of the data!

Really interesting angle focusing on young parents and brand reputation rather than just sales metrics. The choice to track positive comment rates on Meituan and Dianping makes a lot of sense since that's where people actually make dining decisions for their families. I think the keyword tracking around health and cleanliness could surface some really useful insights, especially after how much food safety awareness has grown in China over the past few years. The "Family Meal Review Campaign" idea sounds promising too, though it might be worth thinking about how to keep those reviews feeling genuine instead of overly promotional, since parents on Xiaohongshu can usually tell when something is too scripted. Also curious whether the competitor comparison with KFC will show any major differences, given that they target a pretty similar family demographic in China. Solid plan, would love to see the final infographic!

Good point about CTAs—their comment sections are usually pretty dead unless they're doing a giveaway or asking something specific. I feel like car enthusiast accounts in general struggle with this, people just double-tap and scroll. Maybe leaning into nostalgia content would help? Tons of millennials grew up with Hot Wheels and that emotional angle might get more shares.

The sauce-mixing content idea is actually genius—every time I go to Haidilao there's always someone at the table googling "best Haidilao sauce combo" lol. That stuff is super searchable and shareable. One thing though: for the HK market specifically, you might want to factor in Xiaohongshu since a lot of the 18-34 crowd there uses it more than Instagram for food recs. Also the queue frustration is SO real, I've literally left before because the wait was 90+ mins on a Friday. If they could somehow gamify the waiting experience in content (like "what free stuff can you get while waiting") that could flip a pain point into engagement. Solid plan overall, the 8-week timeline feels realistic too.

The 12-40 age range feels pretty wide tbh—a middle schooler and a 38-year-old office worker probably aren't responding to the same content. Might be worth splitting that into like 2-3 segments when you're analyzing which posts hit with who. Also for HK specifically, Shake Shack lines can get insane during new menu drops, so maybe there's an angle there? Like "is the wait worth it" type content or sneak peeks that build hype before launch. The point about promotion-heavy posts underperforming is super valid though, I always scroll past anything that looks too ad-like. Curious if you'd count IG story polls and quizzes as engagement too since those feel more low-effort for users to interact with.

The off-season drop-off point is really interesting—I never thought about it but you're right, Apple basically goes silent between events and then suddenly it's everywhere again in September. Although tbh part of me wonders if that's intentional? Like the scarcity builds hype. The #ShotOniPhone campaign is lowkey carrying their entire Instagram presence though, it's basically free content from users that also flexes the camera quality. One thing I'd add: sentiment around Apple gets WILD on Twitter whenever there's a controversial change (remember the headphone jack drama? or the butterfly keyboard?). Might be worth separating sentiment by platform since the vibes are very different between IG and X.

This is a really sharp take on why Heytea's winning while everyone else is just slashing prices. The lipstick effect angle makes total sense—people still want their little treats even when they're cutting back elsewhere. Ngl the hand-drawn cup thing completely took over my Xiaohongshu feed last month. Curious if you think this strategy would work for competitors or if it's too late for them to copy without looking desperate?

This proposal is a masterclass in conversion-focused marketing because it refuses to get distracted by "fluff" metrics. By centering everything around CTR (Click-Through Rate) and that "I saw it → I clicked" journey, you’ve turned a fast-food craving into a disciplined sales funnel. I especially love the stakeholder-specific reporting plan—it shows you understand that an executive cares about ROI while a creator just needs to know which "crispy sound" Reel actually makes people hungry.

This blog is incredibly sharp because it exposes the "personality split" Chagee has across borders.You’ve perfectly identified that while Chagee acts like a soulful storyteller on Rednote, its Hong Kong Instagram feels like a "corporate robot" just handing out flyers. Comparing the clinical coldness of Starbucks HK to the cultural warmth of Silk Tea provides a brilliant reality check—it proves that in Hong Kong, local slang and "tea-pairing" stories are what actually drive foot traffic, not just polished product shots. It’s a sophisticated, "boots-on-the-ground" analysis that tells the brand exactly how to stop broadcasting and start belonging.

This plan is brilliant because it looks past the "hype" to find the "gap," accurately calling out that CASETiFY is great at being famous but needs to work on being a "friend." By benchmarking against Wildflower, you’ve hit on the perfect strategy: stop just selling products and start building a lifestyle community that people actually want to talk to, not just buy from. It’s a sophisticated, data-backed reality check that turns "cool" into "community depth."

This plan is incredibly "street smart" because it skips the fluff and goes straight for the conversion. By tracking saves and shares as "planning behavior," you’ve nailed exactly how to turn Shanghai's nightlife hype into actual bookings without overcomplicating things. It’s a sharp, no-nonsense roadmap that treats Douyin like a high-performance sales funnel rather than just a mood board.

This plan is brilliant because it treats digital fashion like a living market, using Sentiment Analysis to bridge the gap between "what looks good" and "what players actually want to pay for." By focusing on Topic Participation and style-specific heatmaps, you've created a direct feedback loop that helps developers avoid "design fatigue" and maximize gacha ROI. It’s a sophisticated blend of subculture insight and hard data that turns virtual photography into actionable business intelligence.

This blog post provides a concise and logical framework for optimizing McDonald's social media strategy by prioritizing engagement over mere exposure. The emphasis on content analysis and data-driven scheduling offers a practical roadmap for effectively capturing the 18-35 demographic.

This analysis provides a sophisticated take on "meme marketing" by focusing on how Mixue's Snow King IP acts as a bridge between viral exposure and long-term brand loyalty. I particularly like the inclusion of network centrality concepts to describe the target audience, as it shows a deep understanding of how Gen Z drives trends.

This proposal captures the unique "punk-meets-luxury" essence of Vivienne Westwood by cleverly linking subcultural expression with modern ethical consumption. The focus on "fashion + activism" is particularly astute, as it aligns the brand's heritage with the values that drive high engagement among the 18–35 demographic today.

This proposal effectively captures the "high-frequency, high-collaboration" essence of Luckin Coffee’s digital-first business model. The decision to use Content Analysis to specifically compare different brand partnerships is a smart move, as Luckin’s success often hinges on which "crossover" goes viral.

This analytics plan for Keeta is impressively pragmatic, specifically in how it bridges the "last mile" between social media engagement and actual app conversions (downloads and first orders). Using external tools like Qi Mai Data to validate internal metrics shows a professional-grade understanding of the competitive food delivery landscape in Hong Kong.

This blog provides a highly structured and professional roadmap for any ethical brand looking to move beyond "aesthetic" posting into data-driven growth.What stands out most is the Applied Trend Analysis. By moving away from generic metrics and focusing on shifting consumer conversations—like "circular fashion" and "upcycled denim"—the plan addresses the biggest challenge in the sustainable sector: staying relevant without compromising brand integrity.

This analysis of Xiaoheihe (Little Black Box) highlights a sophisticated understanding of the information-utility gap in gaming communities. By focusing on "Information Density" and "Cognitive Load" through content analysis, the plan correctly identifies that for a vertical platform, utility is the primary currency of engagement. The transition from text-heavy guides to visually-mapped strategies (flowcharts and screenshots) is a vital shift that aligns with the fast-paced consumption habits of the 18–25 student demographic. By emphasizing problem-solving content over general news, Xiaoheihe positions itself not just as a social app, but as an essential utility in the gamer’s toolkit.

This analysis for Diptyque demonstrates a sophisticated grasp of sensory marketing within a digital framework. By prioritizing "Trend Analysis" over static metrics, the plan cleverly accounts for the seasonal volatility inherent in the luxury fragrance market, particularly around "gifting windows" like Valentine’s Day and Christmas. The focus on "scenario-based displays" and "narrative short videos" is a critical strategic move; it shifts the brand from selling a physical product—which cannot be smelled through a screen—to selling an aspirational lifestyle and an artistic identity. This approach effectively bridges the gap between Parisian heritage and the fast-paced visual culture of platforms like Instagram and Rednote.

This social media analytics plan for Marriott International is a standout example of outcome-oriented strategy, shifting the focus from "vanity metrics" like likes to the "hard currency" of conversion rates and direct bookings. By selecting Competitor Analysis as the primary method, the plan intelligently acknowledges that in the hyper-competitive luxury hospitality sector, success is relative; understanding why a traveler chooses Hilton or Mandarin Oriental over Marriott is the key to capturing market share. The integration of RED (Xiaohongshu) for the Asian market is a critical tactical move, as it recognizes that for affluent travelers in this region, peer-to-peer trust and "lifestyle-first" content often carry more weight than traditional corporate advertising.

This plan for Haidilao is a highly practical and "feet-on-the-ground" strategy that perfectly identifies the price-sensitivity of the Gen Z student demographic. By utilizing Sentiment Analysis, the plan moves beyond just counting likes to understanding the "why" behind the data—specifically identifying that while brand love is high (for service and food), the perceived cost remains a barrier. The "Student Budget Challenge" is a brilliant tactical response; it doesn't just promote a discount, it gamifies the dining experience and provides "social proof" that a luxury hotpot experience can fit a student budget.

Great plan for Pizza Hut’s social media strategy! Super targeted to the 18-30 crowd, with clear goals, comprehensive data tracking and actionable tactics like UGC challenges and IP collabs—perfect for boosting engagement and brand awareness. Love the competitor analysis angle too, it’ll make the strategy even more sharp and effective. Well done!

Awesome Sanrio social media strategy plan! Super targeted to the 15-35 female Gen Z/young millennial audience and their love for kawaii culture, with clear goals centered on boosting brand awareness and engagement. The multi-source data collection (platform insights, third-party tools + user research) is thorough, and content analysis is the perfect call to pinpoint high-resonance post formats. The actionable tactics—content optimization, UGC activation with hashtags/KOLs, and platform-specific content tailoring—are spot-on for building emotional connections with Sanrio’s IPs, way better than just product pushes. Clear KPIs and visual reporting make tracking success straightforward too, great work!

Great strategy for your family-run Thai restaurant! Super local-focused with clear goals to boost brand awareness for your Sydney locations, and the target audience (18-35 students, Sydney’s Muslim community, global visitors) is perfectly tailored to your Kingsford/Haymarket/Newtown spots near UNSW/USYD.

Brilliant data-driven strategy for Blue Bottle Coffee—such a sharp, practical take on ditching guesswork for intentional marketing! Love that you zeroed in on a clear pain point (making sustainability content stick) and tied every step back to the data science lifecycle, with no fluff or vanity metrics in sight.

Fantastic Jellycat social media analytics study—such a thoughtful, brand-aligned strategy that nails what makes this beloved plush brand special! Love the actionable strategies here.This is a very solid framework! I particularly appreciate that you included Xiaohongshu in the data mix—it’s such a critical channel for the 18–35 demographic in Hong Kong right now. Also, using Tableau for the final visualization will definitely make the insights more digestible for stakeholders. Great job connecting the data back to business KPIs. I also agree that a UGC incentive program is the best way to build trust for a brand that positions itself on value and efficiency.

I love the cross-platform approach (Instagram to Xiaohongshu). Since travel is so visual, have you considered incorporating Computer Vision or Image Similarity Analysis into your data collection? Often, what migrates between platforms isn't a specific hashtag, but a specific 'Visual Aesthetic' (e.g., a specific filter style or photo composition). Tracking how a 'visual meme' moves from an IG Reel to a Xiaohongshu post—even if the text language changes—would be a powerful way to trace that 'ripple effect' you mentioned.

The point about collaborating with student micro-influencers is excellent. To make this even more specific, you might want to introduce the concept of 'Nano-Influencers' (1k–10k followers) or 'Campus Ambassadors.' Research often shows that Nano-influencers have significantly higher engagement rates than Micro-influencers because their audience is comprised of actual friends and classmates. For a localized, budget-conscious strategy, targeting specific university campuses via these 'Nano' creators often yields a better ROI than broader influencer campaigns.

This is a strong proposal, particularly the focus on Meituan and Dianping, which are critical for O2O conversion in China. To make the analysis even more actionable, have you considered using Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA)? Instead of just a general 'positive comment rate,' ABSA would allow you to split sentiment into specific buckets like 'Food Safety,' 'Service Speed,' and 'Playground Cleanliness.' A parent might give a 5-star rating for the food but mention a dirty table in the text—ABSA would capture that specific operational gap, which is crucial for the 'Cleanliness' perception you mentioned.

This is a solid foundation! Given Burton's legacy in building snowboard culture, I would suggest expanding the Content Analysis section to specifically compare User-Generated Content (UGC) against Brand-Generated Content. In the outdoor industry, the 'tribe' mentality is massive. Tracking how often regular riders tag Burton in their own clips (using social listening for specific hashtags) might reveal that reposting raw community content drives higher engagement than polished athlete ads. This 'authenticity' is a key currency for your 18-35 demographic.

Solid framework! Since Subway is a physical retail business, a knowledge-building addition to your Google Analytics section would be to look at O2O (Online-to-Offline) Attribution. While tracking website visits is good, tracking 'Coupon Redemptions' from social-exclusive codes is better. If you give a specific digital coupon code only on Instagram Stories, you can directly correlate that social engagement to an actual sandwich sold in-store, closing the loop on ROI.

This is a solid start! Your proposal hits all the key sections needed for a basic analytics plan. To make it even stronger (and sound more like a seasoned marketing student), here are a few brief, constructive tips to level it up:1. Refine the KPI Calculation While “Engagement Rate” is the right KPI, it’s helpful to define how you’re calculating it. In a real study, you’d usually divide total interactions by the number of followers or impressions. Tip: Mention if you are looking at Engagement per Post or Engagement per Follower to show you understand the math behind the metrics. Segment Your Audience “Jobs to be Done”You’ve identified the 18–35 age group, but their motivations on different platforms vary. Briefly note that Xiaohongshu users look for “aesthetic/lifestyle” content, while Weibo users engage more with “trending news/celeb gossip.” Tailoring the data collection to these platform-specific behaviors adds a layer of depth.

I like how you focused specifically on the IP collaborations since that’s such a huge part of Luckin’s current identity. Your choice of Weibo and RED (Xiaohongshu) is spot on because they capture two totally different sides of the brand—the massive "trending" buzz and the "aesthetic" lifestyle reviews. To make this feel a bit more like a deep-dive university project, you might want to clarify how you’ll measure the "Moutai effect" versus a more niche collab like "Tom & Jerry." For example, adding a comparison of "Broad Market IPs" versus "Niche Nostalgia IPs" in your Analysis section would show you’re thinking about the strategy behind the partnerships, not just the visuals. Also, regarding your hypothesis about packaging over price, it would be cool to see if you can track "re-posting" behavior specifically; on RED, users often save or "collect" posts for the aesthetic, which is a very high-value form of engagement compared to just a quick like.

A really cool shift from the Luckin Coffee project, and focusing on that massive December 25th spike for McDonald's makes the proposal feel super current and data-driven. Using Google Trends as a primary KPI is a smart move because it captures "intent" rather than just passive scrolling, which is exactly what a massive brand like McDonald’s needs to track. To build on this from a student perspective, you might want to consider the "Search Intent" behind that 100-score peak. Since it happened on Christmas, a lot of that traffic could be "navigational"—people just checking which locations are open—rather than "discovery" of a new product. In your Analysis section, it would be awesome to differentiate between "Branded Search" (people looking for the store) and "Campaign Search" (people looking for a specific viral menu hack). This shows you aren't just looking at the big number, but actually digging into the quality of that awareness.

For an e-commerce giant like iHerb, where the products are often functional (like vitamins) rather than just "cool," tracking saves is a brilliant way to measure utility over just vanity. Using RED (Xiaohongshu) as a data source is also a great call because that platform’s users are famous for writing "mini-encyclopedias" about ingredient lists, which perfectly matches your focus on institutional trust. To add some extra "student-to-student" insight, you might want to consider how iHerb’s global nature affects your data collection. Since they ship to over 180 countries, the "Sentiment Scores" you mentioned might vary wildly based on local logistics. It would be interesting to see if you can segment your analysis by region to see if a "shipping delay" complaint in one country is drowning out "product quality" praise in another. This would help make your "Strategy & Action" section even more precise by suggesting localized customer service fixes alongside the global marketing vlogs.

Great plan! I love the focus on limited-time items. This actually connects perfectly with GraphSAGE. Since seasonal items are essentially "new nodes", transductive methods like DeepWalk might struggle to predict their performance.
You could try predicting their success by aggregating content features (like visual style), just as GraphSAGE does for unseen nodes. Also, it's worth considering if the "food trends" are driven by Information Cascades—people engaging just because others are. Distinguishing genuine interest from the 'rich-get-richer' effect would really refine your strategy!

Great catch on A Little Tea's 'low-key' vibe! It actually complements your analysis on IP collaborations perfectly.
From a network perspective, this looks like a balanced strategy: IP collaborations expand Weak Ties (increasing Reach), while public welfare campaigns likely cultivate Strong Ties (deepening Loyalty). It would be fascinating to use Community Detection to compare the user clusters of these two content types. This could reveal how the brand balances expanding its audience with maintaining a core, resilient community.

Love the KFC 'Crazy Thursday' comparison! It really highlights the power of ritual.
From a network perspective, KFC is essentially building a 'regular' network structure—fans know exactly when to engage, which naturally forms a dense, loyal community over time. In contrast, McDonald's sporadic product drops function more like a 'random' network. Your analysis effectively illustrates how temporal regularity might be just as critical as content quality for building a sticky social cluster.

Your analysis of Mixue is quite insightful, especially the point about the 'cross-community' effect. It’s a perfect illustration of how a brand can trigger an Information Cascade.
From a network science perspective, the Gen Z audience you identified acts as more than just consumers; their high Betweenness Centrality allows them to function as 'bridges'. This explains how Mixue’s jingle successfully penetrated various dense clusters—like the anime or dance subcultures you mentioned—which are usually resistant to outside information. It seems Mixue has effectively increased the 'payoff' for users to join the trend, where the Direct Benefit of sharing a meme outweighs the effort, making the cascade almost impossible to stop until it reaches equilibrium across the entire network.

I really like how you used Google Trends to see if the social media buzz actually leads to more people searching for Jollibee offline—it's a very practical way to measure 'real' awareness.
Your point about Jollibee’s international growth is interesting. It’s tough because, in the Coordination Game model, people usually need to see a certain fraction of their friends trying a new place before they feel the 'payoff' is high enough to switch from their usual spots. If your trend analysis can identify the exact moment a local cluster hits that adoption threshold, it could be the key to turning an Incomplete Cascade into a full-blown brand takeover in new markets.

I really enjoyed your take on Nike, especially the idea of using Cantonese slogans to connect with local audiences. It’s a great example of how a global brand can feel personal.
From a social psychology perspective, this strategy perfectly aligns with Self-Presentation. When young adults in Hong Kong or Canton share these localized posts, they are often using Nike’s brand identity to manage their own social image—disclosing their cultural pride and athletic lifestyle.
Also, your content analysis could reveal how Nike meets the Affective needs mentioned in the Uses & Gratification Theory. By providing motivational stories rather than just product ads, Nike gives users an emotional 'payoff' that keeps them engaged. It would be cool to see if the data shows that these emotional posts trigger a stronger Information Cascade than the standard product promotions!

Your focus on 'visual storytelling' for Insta360 is spot on. It really highlights how the brand leverages the IC Model. When users see those immersive 360-degree videos, it often triggers Mirroring or Emulation—they don't just want the camera; they want to replicate the cool lifestyle and unique perspectives they see.
With nearly 3 million followers, Insta360’s official account acts as a massive Hub in a Scale-free network. This explains why a single 'topic challenge' can initiate such a widespread cascade so quickly. I also found your mention of 'behind-the-scenes' content interesting; from a U&G Theory perspective, that probably satisfies the Cognitive needs of tech-savvy users who want to know 'how it’s made,' not just the final result. Great job on the formulas!

I really like your choice of Weverse Shop. K-pop fandoms are a fascinating example of what we call Strong Communities in network science—the ties between fans are so dense that information and behaviors propagate almost instantly.
Your focus on unboxing videos and 'photocard sets' is a great link to Reward Learning. For fans, getting an exclusive item stimulates the ventral striatum, providing a huge dopamine hit. However, as you mentioned, negative sentiment regarding logistics can disrupt this 'reward' cycle.
I also find your plan to use 'Sentiment-Content Correlation' quite smart. It could help the brand understand if the Affective needs of the fans are being overshadowed by the frustration of shipping delays. If the sentiment turns too negative, it might even stop the Information Cascade of merch recommendations within the community. Great insights!

I really like your focus on 'information density' for Xiaoheihe. Your observation that visuals 'reduce the effort to take in information' is a great tie-in to the concept of Cognitive Effort. In a high-stakes gaming context, users want to minimize the mental cost of understanding a guide while maximizing their Payoff—which in this case is winning the game!
It’s also interesting to see how those beginner guides function as a trigger for an Informational Cascade. When a new player sees a post with a high number of 'Saves', they use that as 'Social Proof' to reduce their Uncertainty about the game's difficulty. Since Xiaoheihe is such a niche, vertical platform, it feels like a classic Strong Community where internal ties are very dense, making high-quality user-generated strategies the primary currency for engagement.

I really enjoyed your plan for Domino’s, especially the 'New Product Flavor Poll' idea. It’s a great way to move beyond just tracking reach and actually engage the community.
From a network perspective, these polls are like a real-time Coordination Game. When users see others voting for a specific flavor, it reduces their Uncertainty about trying something new, which is a key barrier for Western food in the local market.
Your focus on 'visually appealing food content' also fits perfectly with the Affective needs in the Uses & Gratification Theory. It shows that for your target audience, social media isn't just for information; it's about the emotional 'payoff' of seeing something crave-worthy. Integrating those Dianping and Keeta reviews will definitely help you identify the exact threshold where positive social proof triggers a mass Information Cascade for a new pizza launch.

The target customers align with the brand's core values, and the three major platforms encompass the vast majority of the audience, avoiding the inefficiency of generalized marketing. However, KPIs and target settings need to be more precise. KPI engagement rate only reflects the resonance of the existing audience and cannot measure the brand's ability to reach potential users.

The KPI selects "Engagement Rate" as the core indicator and "Sentiment Score" as the auxiliary indicator. This measures both the content's resonance and brand reputation, forming a dual monitoring system of "interaction quality + brand perception," which is logically sound. Action strategies can incorporate "platform differentiation" design, tailoring activities to the specific characteristics of different platforms.

The plan's overall framework is professional and logically rigorous. The competitor analysis and strategic direction possess strong strategic value and feasibility. The analysis also delves into brand differentiators (such as Apple's privacy and security). Further developments can include risk contingency plans and clearly defined rules for tracking and adjusting subsequent performance to ensure the strategy's effective implementation.

The plan addresses the core pain point of "students being price-sensitive," and its data collection, strategies, and reporting all revolve around the core objective of "guiding students to consume," eliminating redundant steps. However, sentiment analysis is too simplistic and should be replaced with more specific measures. For example, extracting core themes from price-related comments, such as "insufficient discount" or "unfriendly discount period," clarifies whether the core issue is the "discount format" or the "discount amount."

This analysis plan has a precise target customer profile. The analytical methods align with the brand's characteristics, and the reasoning is sound. However, some analyses are slightly absolute. For example, ‘if seasonal campaigns underperform compared to everyday themes, it indicates audiences prefer relevance over festivity.’ Starbucks' seasonal limited-edition campaigns are one of the brand's core selling points, but the aesthetics may not align well with the needs of local working professionals. Combining holiday aesthetics with workplace scenarios could be an alternative.

This analytics plan is an excellent, practical one. The entire plan focuses on customer conversion rates, has a logical closed loop, and a rigorous analytical design. However, on TikTok, it's impossible to distinguish which video the traffic came from from the user's profile page. Therefore, it's advisable to include download links within the videos themselves. This would provide data that more directly reflects which video content customers prefer. Furthermore, the target customer can be further segmented. For example, university students might be more concerned about the price of coffee, while working professionals might prioritize convenience.

As a Nintendo fan who grew up playing games, I strongly agree with the overall approach of this social media analytics solution—emotion, reputation, and fan experience are indeed the core elements that Nintendo should prioritize. This plan captures the most valuable aspects of the Nintendo brand: nostalgia, a sense of companionship, and player trust. However, when analyzing, we can't just look at the positive or negative emotions; we also need to distinguish between "anticipatory complaints" and "genuine dissatisfaction." Often, when we complain about "no new information" or "being crazy about a sequel," it's actually out of deep love and high expectations, not genuine dislike.

As a die-hard Kuromi fan, what impressed me most about this plan was its accurate grasp of the core attributes of Sanrio fans—emotional connection outweighs product consumption—avoiding the pitfall of "only promoting merchandise and ignoring IP sentiment." However, this plan needs to incorporate "IP preference." The fan base for each Sanrio character has significantly different preferences; a uniform approach could easily overlook the needs of individual IP fans. User-generated content (UGC) activities also need to align with the individual IP's persona to enhance fan engagement.

This analysis doesn't blindly apply a generic template. Instead, it designs its framework around Mixue Ice Cream's core competitiveness (e.g., the Snow King IP, affordable positioning), focusing on UGC conversion and long-term brand loyalty, aligning with its brand positioning of "affordable tea drinks + mass-market trends." However, the analysis can be further subdivided. For example, relevant UGC can be categorized into three types: "spontaneous creation (fan-created content, check-in sharing), officially guided creation (challenges, event participation), and KOL-driven creation." The themes of UGC content can be further subdivided (e.g., Snow King fan art, product check-ins, theme song remixes, offline store scenarios) to avoid general analysis and identify user preferences.

This social media analytics solution for Volkswagen Hong Kong has a clear and logical framework, closely centered on the brand's core models (ID.4 and Golf) and target audience, demonstrating both brand understanding and social media strategy. However, objectively speaking, the solution lacks sufficient audience segmentation and fails to reflect group differences. For example, young adults in their 20s may be more interested in automotive technology and handling performance, while families in their 30s may prioritize space, safety, and reliability. These two groups may also differ in their social media content preferences and activity times. The blog's "one-size-fits-all" audience targeting may result in content failing to accurately address the needs of different groups, impacting operational effectiveness. It is recommended to segment the target audience and design differentiated content and publishing strategies for different groups to improve content precision.

This is a well-structured analytics plan with a clear objective and strong alignment between the campaign goal, KPI, and content analysis approach. One interesting extension could be to further leverage the “limited-time” nature of Sushiro’s promotions by incorporating a time-based comparison, such as comparing engagement before, during, and after each campaign.

In addition, since the data is collected from both Instagram and Facebook, it may be valuable to briefly discuss whether engagement patterns differ across platforms and whether the same content category performs consistently on both. This could help refine platform-specific posting strategies.

Lastly, while engagement rate is appropriate, it might be helpful to clarify how follower growth or reach differences across campaigns are controlled, to ensure that engagement comparisons are not influenced by audience size changes over time.

This is an engaging and well-motivated plan, and the choice of Pop Mart fits very naturally with a content analysis approach. One suggestion to strengthen the analytics design would be to narrow down to a single primary KPI, since Reach and Impressions are closely related and may move together. Clarifying which one directly reflects the campaign objective could make the evaluation more focused.

In addition, because Pop Mart operates across multiple platforms (Instagram, Xiaohongshu, TikTok), it might be useful to briefly consider whether the same content categories are evaluated separately per platform, as algorithms and user behaviors can differ significantly.

Finally, since collaborations and product drops often occur in bursts, introducing a simple time-window comparison around major releases could help distinguish short-term hype from sustained brand awareness.

This is a solid and comprehensive plan with a well-justified use of competitor analysis, especially given the comparable positioning of Sushiro, Hama Sushi, and Sushi Express. One potential improvement would be to clarify how differences in posting frequency and account size across competitors are controlled when comparing engagement rates, as these factors may influence results beyond content strategy alone.

Additionally, since both official brand posts and user-generated content are included, it may be useful to briefly distinguish how insights from these two sources are interpreted differently—official posts reflecting brand strategy, and UGC reflecting customer perception. This separation could make the analysis more precise and actionable.

Overall, explicitly addressing these aspects would further strengthen the validity and interpretability of the competitor comparison.

This is a thoughtfully designed plan, especially in how it connects Citybus’s rebranding goal with competitor benchmarking against KMB’s more playful communication style. One aspect that could further strengthen the analysis is to explicitly distinguish between functional engagement and brand-level engagement, since a portion of Citybus’s interactions (e.g., delay inquiries or route questions) may reflect service dependency rather than brand affinity.

In addition, because public transport usage is often influenced by external constraints rather than personal preference, it may be useful to clarify how engagement metrics are interpreted in the context of a quasi-monopoly market. For example, considering sentiment polarity or comment intent alongside engagement rate could help separate operational feedback from genuine brand resonance.

This clarification would make the competitor comparison more robust and better aligned with the rebranding objective.

This is a very strong and insightful analysis, particularly in how it links Chagee’s regional storytelling success on RedNote to the missed opportunity on Instagram in Hong Kong. One area that could further strengthen the analytics plan is to clarify how causal impact is inferred, as higher engagement from localized storytelling may also be influenced by factors such as store proximity, offline promotions, or influencer exposure.

In addition, since the goal is framed as lead generation rather than pure engagement, it may be useful to briefly outline how engagement signals (e.g., saves, comments mentioning locations, or DM inquiries) could be mapped to proxy indicators of offline foot traffic. This would help align the storytelling strategy more tightly with the business objective.

Overall, explicitly connecting cultural storytelling metrics to measurable conversion proxies would make the strategy both analytically rigorous and execution-ready.

This blog presents a well-structured and conceptually strong analysis of Heytea’s shift from product-driven positioning to emotional asset cultivation. I particularly appreciate the move from simple traffic-based KPIs toward UGC as a higher-order engagement metric, as it reflects co-creation rather than passive consumption. However, one critical question remains underexplored: whether UGC volume truly translates into sustainable brand equity or merely reflects short-term virality shaped by platform algorithms. Emotional participation can generate bursts of visibility, but without linking UGC to repeat purchase behavior, retention rates, or long-term LTV, it risks becoming symbolic engagement rather than economic value. To strengthen the study, I would suggest incorporating conversion-based or cohort-level analysis to examine whether emotional engagement drives durable loyalty beyond social expression. This would make the “emotional moat” argument more empirically convincing rather than primarily narrative-driven.

This is a well-structured and practical analytics plan, especially in how it categorizes aesthetic styles and links sentiment insights to gacha scheduling decisions. I also appreciate the cross-market perspective between domestic and global platforms. One possible extension to further strengthen the proposal might be connecting social media sentiment with in-game behavioral data. For example, it would be interesting to examine whether highly discussed clothing styles also demonstrate stronger purchase conversion or repeat participation in gacha events. By combining external engagement signals with internal monetization metrics, the strategy could become even more data-driven and actionable. Overall, this is a thoughtful plan with clear product implications.

This proposal is very clear, structured, and practical, especially in how it links content formats directly to CTR and aligns reporting with different stakeholders’ needs. One possible extension could be to consider adding a secondary metric such as conversion rate (CTR → actual orders) to complement the analysis. In food delivery scenarios, clicks may sometimes reflect curiosity rather than purchase intent, so observing the drop-off between landing page visits and completed orders might generate additional insight. Overall, this is a strong, action-oriented analytics plan with a realistic marketing mindset.

This proposal thoughtfully captures Bilibili’s transition from a niche ACG platform to a broader pan-entertainment ecosystem, especially through the concept of cross-category penetration and interactive retention. Balancing growth with community identity could make the analytics framework even more strategically aligned with Bilibili’s unique ecosystem.

This research plan provides a clear and practical observational framework, especially in how it connects engagement patterns with specific campaign formats such as collaborations, live streams and DIY activities. While observational data offers valuable insights into performance trends, adding light comparative elements could strengthen the explanatory power of the findings. Overall, this is a thoughtful and well-structured proposal with strong strategic relevance.

Thanks for pointing this out. You’re right that the metric definition should be clearer. Since regular viewers on Xiaohongshu usually cannot see views or impressions, I would probably use visible engagement data like likes, comments, and saves to compare post performance. I agree that this limitation should be acknowledged in the method section, and the analysis could be more rigorous if account-level data were available.

Thanks for highlighting this point. I also think the distinction between informational and lifestyle content is important, especially for a new F&B brand where consumers may still be uncertain about store locations, menu options, or prices. On Xiaohongshu, a high number of saves often suggests that users want to revisit the post later, which may indicate planning behavior before a purchase. That’s why I thought transforming high-save posts into a “newcomer guide” or pinned series could help reduce this uncertainty and make it easier for first-time customers to visit the store.

Thanks for pointing this out. I agree that comment sections can be a valuable source of user insight, especially for a brand that is still relatively new in a market. Repeated questions about things like store locations, menu options, or promotions can reveal what information potential customers are missing before deciding to visit. By identifying these patterns, the brand can design posts that directly address those uncertainties, which is why I suggested formats like a “newcomer guide.” This way, content analysis can translate more directly into practical communication strategies.

Thanks for the comment! I also think the “save” metric can be a useful signal for potential purchase interest on Xiaohongshu, since users often save posts they want to revisit later, such as store recommendations or menu suggestions. Of course, a save doesn’t necessarily guarantee a future purchase, but it may indicate stronger intent compared to simple likes. That’s why I considered using saves to identify high-utility content and then turning those posts into formats like a “newcomer guide.” If those posts gain traction, they could indeed help drive more offline visits to the store.

Thanks for your thoughtful feedback. I agree that engagement can be more meaningful than simple reach on a discovery-based platform like Xiaohongshu, where interaction often reflects whether the content is actually useful or interesting to users. For a food brand, posts that trigger comments, saves, or discussions may indicate stronger curiosity or purchase consideration. That’s also why I suggested turning high-performing formats into repeatable content series like a “newcomer guide,” so the brand can build a more consistent and recognizable content structure over time.
